title: Epic: Make all version strings dynamic and self-consistent
description: Currently, 5+ locations in the codebase hard-coded version strings like 1.0.0, 0.1.0, or 1.0 instead of deriving from CARGO_PKG_VERSION. This session fixed the known instances (commit 12a6401), but the underlying pattern needs a systematic guard to prevent regression. When the package version in Cargo.toml changes (e.g., 0.1.0 to 0.2.0), hard-coded version strings silently become wrong. For a triage engine where determinism and trust are core values, emitting incorrect version metadata undermines the tool credibility. This epic covers adding CI-level guards and documentation to prevent future regressions.

title: Add grep-based CI check that catches hard-coded version strings
description:
Add a CI step (or a cargo test) that greps the src/ directory for literal version strings that should instead use env!(CARGO_PKG_VERSION). The check should flag patterns like '"1.0.0"' or '"0.1.0"' in non-test Rust code that appear in version-like contexts (struct fields named 'version', 'schema_version', etc.).

IMPLEMENTATION APPROACH:
- Preferred: a #[test] function in lib.rs or a dedicated test file that uses include_str! or reads src/ files and asserts no hard-coded version patterns exist outside of test code
- Alternative: a shell script in scripts/ invoked by CI that greps for suspicious patterns
- The test should allowlist known safe uses (e.g., the BLURB_VERSION HTML markers in agents.rs which are guarded by the agent_blurb_version_matches_constant test)

WHY THIS MATTERS:
Five instances of hard-coded versions were found and fixed in commit 12a6401. Without a guard, the same mistake will recur the next time someone adds a new struct with a version field. The robot envelope, pages meta, triage meta, and schema structs all need version fields — its easy to forget env!() when writing new code.

FILES TO CHECK:
- src/analysis/triage.rs (TriageMeta.version)
- src/robot.rs (RobotSchemas.schema_version, RobotEnvelope.version)
- src/export_pages.rs (PagesMeta.version)
- src/analysis/recipe.rs (default_version())
- src/analysis/brief.rs (BriefMeta.version)
- Any new file that adds a version/schema_version field

title: Extract BLURB_VERSION into the agent blurb marker string dynamically
description:
In agents.rs, BLURB_VERSION (line 15) is a u32 constant set to 1, but the AGENT_BLURB raw string literal (line 25) hard-codes the marker as '<!-- bv-agent-instructions-v1 -->'. When BLURB_VERSION is bumped to 2, someone must manually update the marker inside the raw string — which is easy to forget.

CURRENT STATE:
- A guard test (agent_blurb_version_matches_constant) was added in commit 12a6401 that will catch the mismatch at test time
- But the ideal fix is to construct the blurb dynamically so the version cant drift

CHALLENGE:
AGENT_BLURB is a const &str (raw string literal), which cant contain format!() or concat!(). Options:
1. Use lazy_static! or std::sync::LazyLock to construct the blurb at runtime with the version interpolated
2. Use a const fn that concatenates using const_format crate
3. Use a build.rs step that generates the blurb with the correct version
4. Keep the current approach (const raw string + guard test) and accept the manual sync — the test makes it safe

RECOMMENDATION:
Option 4 (keep guard test) is simplest and already implemented. Only upgrade to option 1/2/3 if the version needs to change frequently. Document the coupling in a code comment near BLURB_VERSION.

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text: Tests pass now; transient rch worker issue

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text: Root cause: rch worker /tmp on btrfs has a space constraint (ENOSPC errno 28). All 18 failing tests use tempfile::tempdir() which defaults to /tmp. Fix: set TMPDIR=/data/tmp when running via rch. All 597 tests pass with this workaround. Permanent fix: update CI config or rch hook to set TMPDIR.

title: Robot output shape parity: close all JSON field gaps vs Go bv
description: Close every JSON output field gap between Go bv and Rust bvr robot commands. The parity audit (2026-03-07) found that while all 38 commands exist and pass conformance tests, several Go output FIELDS are missing in Rust. Downstream AI agents and automation scripts parse these fields, so field-level parity matters. Go reference: legacy_beads_viewer_code/beads_viewer/cmd/bv/main.go lines 2630-3045. Rust outputs: src/main.rs lines 4383-4735, src/robot.rs.
close_reason: All sub-beads complete: bd-1ru.1 (RobotEnvelope), bd-1ru.2 (as_of/as_of_commit), bd-1ru.3 (analysis_config), bd-1ru.4 (label_scope/label_context), bd-1ru.5 (full_stats), bd-1ru.6 (plan reasons). Robot output JSON shape now matches Go bv.

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text: CONTEXT: Go's bv outputs rich JSON envelopes with metadata fields (output_format, version, as_of, as_of_commit, analysis_config, label_scope, full_stats, reason, top_what_ifs, advanced_insights, feedback). Our Rust port produces correct data but ships a simpler JSON shape. This epic adds every missing field so that downstream consumers (scripts, dashboards, other agents) get identical JSON schemas from bvr as from bv. ORDER: Start with bd-1ru.1 (envelope) and bd-1ru.2 (timestamps) as they're universal. Then bd-1ru.3-6 can parallelize. bd-1ru.7-9 depend on analysis engine work in Epic 2.

title: Add output_format and version to generic RobotEnvelope struct
description: Go's RobotEnvelope includes output_format (json|toon) and version fields. Rust's generic RobotEnvelope in src/robot.rs only has generated_at and data_hash. Individual command outputs (forecast, capacity, burndown) added these fields ad-hoc, but the generic envelope should have them so ALL commands get them automatically. IMPLEMENTATION: Add output_format: String and version: String to RobotEnvelope struct in src/robot.rs:15. Update envelope() constructor to populate them. Remove the per-command duplicates from RobotForecastOutput, RobotCapacityOutput, RobotBurndownOutput etc (or keep for backward compat and just ensure they match). Update conformance tests to validate presence. ACCEPTANCE: Every robot command's JSON includes output_format and version at the top level.
close_reason: Refactored all 35 robot output structs to use shared RobotEnvelope with #[serde(flatten)]. Extended envelope with output_format and version fields. Added envelope_empty() for commands without issues. All 630 tests pass.

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text: Go reference: pkg/robot/envelope.go — RobotEnvelope{OutputFormat, Version, GeneratedAt}. We already added output_format+version to some structs (bd-1sx, bd-v3z) but it should be a generic wrapper, not per-struct fields. Consider a RobotEnvelope<T> generic that wraps any robot output with standard metadata. This is the foundation for all other robot output work.

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text: Go reference VERIFIED: cmd/bv/main.go lines 7414-7421 defines RobotEnvelope with GeneratedAt, DataHash, OutputFormat, Version fields. All robot output structs EMBED this via Go struct embedding. The Rust equivalent should be a generic wrapper struct or trait, replacing the 30+ individual output_format/version field repetitions currently in main.rs. This is a DRY refactor that also ensures new robot outputs automatically get envelope fields.

Tests must be in the same file as the implementation.

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text: IMPLEMENTATION NOTE: Use Rust's #[serde(flatten)] attribute for the envelope — this is the Rust equivalent of Go's struct embedding. Define: struct RobotEnvelope { generated_at: String, data_hash: String, output_format: String, version: String }. Then each output struct uses: #[serde(flatten)] pub envelope: RobotEnvelope. This replaces all 30+ output_format/version field repetitions in main.rs.

title: Add as_of and as_of_commit fields to snapshot-aware robot outputs
description: Go's triage, next, plan, insights, and priority robot outputs include as_of (string, e.g. 'HEAD~30') and as_of_commit (resolved git SHA) fields. These enable historical snapshot queries where the user passes --as-of to view a past state. Rust outputs lack these fields entirely. IMPLEMENTATION: (1) Add --as-of CLI flag to cli.rs if not present. (2) Add as_of: Option<String> and as_of_commit: Option<String> to the output structs in main.rs for robot-triage (~line 4383), robot-next (~line 4391), robot-plan (~line 4405), robot-insights (~line 4414), robot-priority (~line 4490). (3) When --as-of is provided, resolve the git ref to a SHA and populate both fields. (4) When not provided, emit null/omit. Go reference: main.go lines 2976 (next), 3009 (triage), 2710 (plan), 2630 (insights), 2822 (priority). ACCEPTANCE: Passing --as-of HEAD~5 populates both fields in all 5 outputs. When omitted, fields are absent or null.
close_reason: Added as_of/as_of_commit fields to RobotTriageOutput, RobotNextOutput, RobotPlanOutput, RobotInsightsOutput, RobotPriorityOutput with resolve_as_of() helper. All 673 tests pass.

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text: Go reference: pkg/robot/snapshot.go — as_of is the timestamp of the data snapshot (when issues were loaded), as_of_commit is the git commit hash at load time. Both are critical for reproducibility — 'this triage was computed against commit abc123 at time T'. Use vergen-gix build info for commit hash, chrono::Utc::now() for as_of.

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text: CURRENT STATE: The --as-of CLI flag already exists and loads issues at a git revision. What's MISSING is including as_of (the revision ref/timestamp) and as_of_commit (the resolved SHA) in the robot OUTPUT structs so consumers know what snapshot the data represents. These fields should be populated from the CLI arg and the resolved git commit when --as-of is used, and omitted (serde skip_serializing_if) when running on current data.

title: CLI/Model unit tests + e2e validation
description: WHAT: Test coverage for all CLI and model changes. (1) Unit tests: exit code verification for --check-drift (mock scenarios returning 0/1/2), BV_PRETTY_JSON env var produces indented output, --related-min-relevance and --related-max-results filter correctly, DateTime<Utc> serialization round-trips through JSON/YAML/TOON, is_closed() vs is_tombstone() return correct booleans for all status values, content_hash computed correctly for known inputs, external_ref stored and retrieved. LOGGING: Each test logs the command invoked, env vars set, expected outcome, actual outcome. On failure, dump stderr and the full CLI help output for context. EDGE CASES: Empty input, Unicode in issue titles, zero-length content_hash input, future timestamps, epoch 0 timestamps. ACCEPTANCE: All flag changes have both positive and negative test cases. E2e script exits 0. PARENT: bd-36l

title: Legacy capability parity recovery program: replace stale completion claims with verified coverage
description:
Background:
The currently closed parity umbrellas and docs claim a level of completion that we cannot responsibly assert from the present evidence. The codebase is strong and broad, but the project still needs a disciplined capability-parity program that distinguishes between implemented behavior, tested behavior, and merely documented intent.

Goal:
Create the canonical issue tree for getting `bvr` to a truthful state where it can do everything legacy `bv` can do for real operator and agent workflows, while allowing Rust-only improvements and better TUI presentation. Pixel matching is explicitly not the goal; workflow, behavior, and operator outcomes are.

Scope:
This epic covers parity auditing, implementation of newly discovered gaps, proof harness expansion, and documentation truthfulness. Each child should be self-contained enough that a future agent can work from the bead alone without reopening old planning markdown.

Acceptance:
1. The parity claim is backed by evidence instead of optimism.
2. Core legacy workflows are either implemented, proven equivalent, or explicitly de-scoped with rationale.
3. The final tracker tree itself is sufficient as the project plan.
close_reason: Legacy capability parity recovery program COMPLETE. All tracks closed: audit (track 1), robot/CLI (track 2), TUI (track 3), export/pages/workspace (track 4), docs/verification (track 5). 271 beads closed.

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text:
Program note:
This tree intentionally supersedes the stale "full parity achieved" narrative. Use it to answer a narrower but more honest question: can `bvr` do every job legacy `bv` can do for robot users and terminal operators? If yes, prove it. If not, create or link a concrete fix bead. Visual improvement is allowed; unsupported workflow regressions are not.

title: Build the parity baseline and gap ledger from code and real workflows
description:
Background:
Before changing more code, we need a fresh baseline that compares what legacy `bv` actually does against what `bvr` demonstrably does today. The previous parity closure mixed real evidence with forward-looking assertions.

Goal:
Turn the tracker into the new source of truth for parity status. This track should inventory legacy workflows, define evidence standards, and record a concrete gap ledger that downstream implementation tracks can execute against.

Acceptance:
- The tracker contains a capability matrix spanning robot CLI, loader/model semantics, TUI workflows, and export/pages integrations.
- Every gap has a child bead or an explicit de-scope rationale.
- The final matrix distinguishes implemented, proven, partial, divergent-by-design, and unknown states.
close_reason: Capability inventory, evidence standard, and fresh gap ledger are now recorded in tracker notes/comments; execution can proceed on robot, TUI, and integration audit tracks

title: Inventory legacy capability surfaces and map them to concrete bvr modules and workflows
description:
Purpose:
Create the canonical capability inventory that downstream beads rely on. This should enumerate, in tracker form, the legacy surfaces that matter: loader semantics, robot outputs, TUI operator workflows, export/pages flows, workspace aggregation, hooks, preview/watch loops, and path/config resolution behavior.

What to capture:
- The user-visible workflow or operator goal.
- The legacy implementation anchor (docs, code area, or fixture).
- The corresponding Rust modules and tests.
- Whether current status appears proven, partial, unknown, or divergent.

Done means:
Future agents can understand the full scope of parity work from the tracker alone.
notes:
Capability inventory snapshot:

1. Data model and ingest semantics
- Legacy workflow: load issue data correctly from `.beads`, preserve status/dependency semantics, ignore malformed records safely, and resolve project/workspace roots the same way legacy `bv` does.
- Rust module anchors: `src/model.rs`, `src/loader.rs`, `src/error.rs`, `src/cli.rs`, `src/main.rs`.
- Current proof anchors: `tests/cli_model_validation.rs`, `tests/test_parse_timestamp.rs`, conformance fixtures, workspace/history integration tests.
- Provisional status for planning: partially proven. Broad coverage exists, but this family remains high risk because many recent parity bugs were really path/preference/resolution mismatches.

2. Graph analysis and recommendation engine
- Legacy workflow: build the blocking-dependency graph and derive triage/plan/insights/forecast/suggest/history outputs from correct semantics.
- Rust module anchors: `src/analysis/mod.rs`, `graph.rs`, `triage.rs`, `plan.rs`, `forecast.rs`, `alerts.rs`, `suggest.rs`, `diff.rs`, `history.rs`, `git_history.rs`, `label_intel.rs`, `search.rs`, `recipe.rs`, `correlation.rs`, `causal.rs`, `drift.rs`, `whatif.rs`, `advanced.rs`, `brief.rs`, `cache.rs`.
- Current proof anchors: `tests/conformance.rs`, `tests/schema_validation.rs`, `tests/e2e_robot_matrix.rs`, `tests/stress_fixtures.rs`, module-local unit tests.
- Provisional status for planning: mostly proven for core families, but some non-core or recently touched workflow surfaces still need fresh-audit confirmation rather than trusting stale parity prose.

3. Robot/CLI contract surface
- Legacy workflow: agents invoke deterministic `--robot-*` commands and get the right payload shape, defaults, filters, sorting, omission rules, and error behavior.
- Rust module anchors: `src/main.rs`, `src/cli.rs`, `src/robot.rs`, plus all analysis modules above.
- Covered command families seen in repo/docs: triage/next/plan/insights/priority/diff/history/forecast/capacity/burndown/graph/alerts/suggest/search/label-health/label-flow/label-attention/recipes/docs/schema/sprint surfaces/metrics/feedback/drift/correlation-impact/file-intel/workflow briefs.
- Current proof anchors: `tests/conformance.rs`, `tests/schema_validation.rs`, `tests/e2e_robot_matrix.rs`, `tests/robot_alerts.rs`, `tests/robot_burndown_scope.rs`, `tests/robot_history_since.rs`, `tests/admin_cli.rs`, `tests/background_mode.rs`, `tests/cli_model_validation.rs`.
- Provisional status for planning: substantial coverage exists, but audit must verify semantics-by-command rather than trusting the parity ledger.

4. Human TUI workflow surface
- Legacy workflow: users can inspect, search, filter, prioritize, explore dependencies/history, and switch views efficiently in a keyboard-first terminal workflow.
- Rust module anchors: `src/tui.rs`, `src/main.rs`, embedded snapshots under `src/snapshots/`.
- Core modes explicitly present in repo/docs: Main, Board, Insights, Graph, History, plus Rust-only modes Actionable, Attention, Tree, LabelDashboard, FlowMatrix, TimeTravelDiff, Sprint.
- Current proof anchors: lib unit tests in `src/tui.rs`, snapshot files, keyflow journeys, e2e journey snapshots, `README.md` key map, `FEATURE_PARITY.md` TUI sections.
- Provisional status for planning: core functionality is broad, but capability parity still has to be judged at the operator-workflow level rather than by keybinding counts or snapshot existence.

5. Export, pages, preview, watch, and deploy workflows
- Legacy workflow: export artifacts, preview server, watch/live-reload loops, and pages wizard/deploy flows work reliably for operators and generate correct bundle/runtime behavior.
- Rust module anchors: `src/export_md.rs`, `src/export_sqlite.rs`, `src/export_pages.rs`, `src/pages_wizard.rs`, `src/viewer_assets.rs`, `src/main.rs`.
- Current proof anchors: `tests/export_md.rs`, `tests/export_pages.rs`, `tests/e2e_export_pages.rs`, `scripts/e2e_preview_pages.sh`.
- Provisional status for planning: implemented and actively tested, but still a high-value audit target because bundle/runtime and failure-path semantics are easy to overclaim.

6. Workspace, hook, repo-path, and cwd-sensitive integration semantics
- Legacy workflow: running from workspace root, nested repo, alternate cwd, explicit repo path, or explicit beads file still uses the intended project/config/hooks/history roots.
- Rust module anchors: `src/loader.rs`, `src/main.rs`, `src/export_md.rs`, `src/export_pages.rs`, `src/pages_wizard.rs`.
- Current proof anchors: `tests/e2e_workspace_history.rs`, `tests/cli_model_validation.rs`, `tests/export_md.rs`, `tests/export_pages.rs`.
- Provisional status for planning: partially proven and especially important. Recent real bugs came from this family, so the new audit should treat it as a root-cause category rather than a side detail.

7. Operational and release surfaces
- Legacy workflow: admin/agent flows, background mode, profiling/metrics, CI verification, and artifact-oriented debugging are available and understandable.
- Rust module anchors: `src/agents.rs`, `src/main.rs`, `build.rs`, `.github/workflows/ci.yml`.
- Current proof anchors: `tests/admin_cli.rs`, `tests/background_mode.rs`, CI workflow, benchmark harness, release/profile docs.
- Provisional status for planning: mixed. Some surfaces are Rust-specific and should be documented as additive, not confused with strict legacy parity.

Planning implication:
The inventory supports the current track split: baseline/audit, robot CLI, TUI workflows, integration/export/workspace, and final evidence/docs. The highest-risk cross-cutting theme is path/root-resolution correctness; the highest-risk truthfulness theme is stale documentation that treats broad implementation as proven equivalence.
close_reason: Capability inventory now recorded in tracker notes/comments with module anchors, proof anchors, and provisional status across all major legacy workflow families

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text:
Inventory recorded:
I mapped the current project into seven capability families with module anchors, proof anchors, and provisional planning status: data ingest/model semantics; graph analysis engine; robot/CLI contract surface; human TUI workflows; export/pages/preview/watch/deploy workflows; workspace/hook/repo-path/cwd-sensitive integration semantics; and operational/release surfaces. The inventory explicitly flags path/root-resolution as the highest-risk cross-cutting category and warns that the current docs overstate proof in several areas.

title: Define the evidence standard for parity claims so beads can be closed honestly
description:
Purpose:
Define what counts as sufficient evidence for saying a surface is done. Some parity claims can be established by fixture-backed conformance; others need TUI journey coverage, shell e2e, or targeted regression tests.

Acceptance criteria:
- Different evidence classes are spelled out for robot, TUI, export/pages, and workspace/hook surfaces.
- The plan distinguishes proof of implementation from proof of behavioral equivalence.
- Closing guidance is strict enough that we do not recreate the current overclaim problem.
notes:
Parity evidence standard:

A. General rule
- A parity claim is valid only when the tracker can point to both the implementation anchor and the proof anchor.
- "Implemented" is not enough. We distinguish: implemented, proven equivalent, partially proven, divergent-by-design, and unknown.
- If the behavior affects operator recovery, path resolution, or cross-mode/context continuity, pure happy-path testing is insufficient.

B. Evidence ladder for loader/model/path semantics
- Required minimum: focused unit tests for parsing/validation rules plus CLI-entry integration coverage for cwd/repo/workspace/beads-file resolution semantics.
- Needed when legacy behavior is subtle: adversarial fixtures or integration cases that exercise malformed input, missing files, ambiguous roots, and precedence rules.
- Closure bar: a future agent can tell which root/path semantics are guaranteed and which are merely inferred.

C. Evidence ladder for robot/CLI contract parity
- Required minimum: one deterministic proof layer near the implementation (unit/conformance/schema) and one proof layer from the real CLI entrypoint.
- Use conformance fixtures when legacy output shape/order/omission is the source of truth.
- Use schema validation when shape contracts matter beyond a single fixture.
- Use CLI integration/e2e when path/config resolution, error behavior, or multi-flag interaction matters.
- Closure bar: command family behavior, default behavior, filter behavior, and failure behavior are all evidenced at the right layer.

D. Evidence ladder for TUI workflow parity
- Required minimum: state/keyflow coverage for the interaction logic plus either snapshot or journey evidence for the user-visible workflow.
- Use snapshots when layout/detail visibility matters.
- Use keyflow/state tests when search/filter/focus/selection transitions matter.
- Use multi-mode journey tests when the operator workflow crosses views or depends on sustained context.
- Closure bar: we can explain not just that a key exists, but that the operator can complete the legacy job-to-be-done.

E. Evidence ladder for export/pages/workspace integration parity
- Required minimum: targeted unit/integration coverage for root-cause semantics plus shell/e2e proof for operator workflows with detailed logs.
- Required logging: cwd/repo/workspace context, replay command, stdout/stderr, and any preview/watch lifecycle artifacts relevant to diagnosis.
- Use failure-path cases deliberately: invalid saved config, broken hook, non-repo cwd, watch reload edge cases, preview path traversal protections, and deploy-target repair flows.
- Closure bar: both happy path and operator recovery path are evidenced.

F. Documentation truthfulness standard
- Docs may only claim parity at the strongest evidence level actually achieved.
- Additive Rust-only features must be labeled as additive, not counted as proof that a legacy workflow is covered.
- Intentional divergences must name the preserved user outcome and why the difference is acceptable.

G. Anti-overclaim rule
- A bead cannot close by citing only old markdown prose or broad suite counts. It must name the specific tests, fixtures, scripts, or artifacts that establish confidence for that surface.
- If evidence is missing but the code looks plausible, classify as partial or unknown until proof exists.

H. Diagnostic quality standard
- Tests and scripts used as parity evidence must fail loudly and locally. A future agent should be able to infer the broken workflow, command family, mode, path context, or fixture from the failure output without rereading the implementation.
close_reason: Tracker now records the parity evidence standard, including required proof ladders and the anti-overclaim rule for closing beads

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text:
Evidence standard recorded:
Parity closure now requires paired implementation and proof anchors, with explicit ladders for loader/path semantics, robot CLI contracts, TUI workflows, export/pages/workspace integrations, and documentation truthfulness. The anti-overclaim rule is explicit: no bead closes on broad suite counts or optimistic prose alone.

title: Record the fresh gap ledger with explicit statuses: proven, partial, unknown, divergent-by-design
description:
Purpose:
Translate the inventory and evidence standard into an actionable gap ledger. This is the gate between planning and implementation.

Requirements:
- Every material gap becomes a child bead in one of the execution tracks.
- Every currently claimed-complete surface is either revalidated or downgraded.
- Any intentional divergence must include why it is acceptable and what user outcome remains covered.
notes:
Fresh gap ledger snapshot:

1. Loader/model/root-resolution semantics
- Status: partial / high-risk.
- Why: recent real bugs in feedback storage, hook cwd, preview path handling, wizard repair routing, and repo-path/beads-file inference all came from this family.
- Existing execution beads covering it: `bd-2bpl.2.1`, `bd-2bpl.2.3.1`, `bd-2bpl.4.3`, `bd-2bpl.4.4.3`, `bd-2bpl.4.5.1`, `bd-2bpl.4.5.2`.

2. Core robot command families (triage/next/plan/priority/insights/diff/history/forecast/capacity/burndown/suggest/graph)
- Status: implemented and broadly proven, but not yet re-audited under the new evidence standard. Treat as provisionally partial until command-by-command audit confirms defaults, filters, omission rules, and failure behavior.
- Existing execution beads covering it: `bd-2bpl.2.2`, `bd-2bpl.2.3.2`, `bd-2bpl.2.4.1`, `bd-2bpl.2.4.2`.

3. Extended/additive robot surfaces (search, label intel, recipes, docs/schema, metrics, feedback, drift, correlation/impact, file-intel, briefs, sprint helpers)
- Status: mixed. Some are additive Rust improvements rather than strict legacy surfaces; others are claimed complete in docs but need truthfulness review so additive does not get confused with parity proof.
- Existing execution beads covering it: `bd-2bpl.2.2`, `bd-2bpl.5.1`.

4. Main TUI workflow parity
- Status: partial / unproven at operator-workflow level. Existing snapshots and keybindings are not enough by themselves to prove users can complete the same browsing, prioritization, and detail-inspection tasks as in legacy `bv`.
- Existing execution beads covering it: `bd-2bpl.3.1`, `bd-2bpl.3.4.1`, `bd-2bpl.3.5.1`, `bd-2bpl.3.5.2`.

5. Board/insights/graph TUI workflow parity
- Status: partial / unproven at workflow level. The modes are rich and heavily tested, but the new standard requires proof of operator jobs-to-be-done rather than mode existence or keybinding coverage.
- Existing execution beads covering it: `bd-2bpl.3.2`, `bd-2bpl.3.4.2`, `bd-2bpl.3.5.1`, `bd-2bpl.3.5.2`.

6. History-mode and cross-mode investigative parity
- Status: partial / high-value audit target. History is specifically vulnerable to overclaim because individual features can exist without the full investigative loop feeling complete.
- Existing execution beads covering it: `bd-2bpl.3.3`, `bd-2bpl.3.4.3`, `bd-2bpl.3.5.1`, `bd-2bpl.3.5.2`.

7. Export/pages/preview/watch/wizard workflows
- Status: implemented and actively tested, but still provisional under the new standard because happy-path coverage and feature presence do not automatically prove workflow equivalence or recovery-path clarity.
- Existing execution beads covering it: `bd-2bpl.4.1`, `bd-2bpl.4.2`, `bd-2bpl.4.4.1`, `bd-2bpl.4.4.2`, `bd-2bpl.4.5.1`, `bd-2bpl.4.5.2`.

8. Documentation and release-readiness claims
- Status: incorrect/overstated in current form. README and FEATURE_PARITY contain strong completion language that now must be treated as claims to be re-earned, not evidence.
- Existing execution beads covering it: `bd-2bpl.5.1`, `bd-2bpl.5.2`.

9. Divergent-by-design/additive surfaces
- Status: divergent-by-design where clearly Rust-specific and not required for legacy parity, but must be labeled honestly. Examples: Rust-only TUI modes, schema/docs outputs, some metrics/reporting differences.
- Existing execution beads covering it: `bd-2bpl.5.1` plus whatever audit bead owns the adjacent workflow family.

Planning conclusion
- No additional top-level tracks are needed right now; the current `bd-2bpl.2` through `.5` split is sufficient.
- The first implementation-phase priorities should stay audit-first: `bd-2bpl.2.1`, `bd-2bpl.2.2`, `bd-2bpl.3.1`, `bd-2bpl.3.2`, `bd-2bpl.3.3`, `bd-2bpl.4.1`, `bd-2bpl.4.2`, and `bd-2bpl.4.3`.
- The strongest current downgrade is this: broad implementation exists, but exact capability parity is not yet proven under the new evidence standard.
close_reason: Fresh gap ledger recorded in tracker notes/comments, with provisional statuses and explicit mapping from each gap family to the current execution beads

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text:
Gap ledger recorded:
The tracker now explicitly downgrades several surfaces from implicit "done" to provisional partial status until fresh audit evidence exists. The biggest current risk classes are path/root-resolution semantics, operator-workflow proof for the TUI, export/pages recovery-path behavior, and documentation truthfulness. No new top-level tracks are needed; the current execution tree is sufficient.

title: Audit and close robot/CLI parity gaps against legacy bv semantics
description:
Background:
Robot-mode parity is the most objective and automation-critical surface. Even small path-resolution, filtering, metadata-shape, or loader-semantic mismatches can break downstream agent workflows.

Goal:
Systematically re-audit all legacy robot and CLI behaviors against the Rust port, then fix any remaining gaps in semantics, outputs, or workflow compatibility.

Acceptance:
- Loader, model, and path-resolution semantics are rechecked against legacy behavior.
- Every legacy robot command family has explicit parity evidence or a documented intentional divergence.
- Integration and conformance coverage exists for all reopened gaps.
close_reason: All child tracks complete: audit (2.1, 2.2), fixes (2.3, 2.3.1, 2.3.2), and test expansion (2.4, 2.4.1, 2.4.2). Robot/CLI parity track fully closed with 30+ bugs fixed, 120+ regression tests, and shell e2e harness.

title: Re-audit loader, model, and path-resolution semantics against legacy behavior
description:
Purpose:
Audit the subtle compatibility layer that everything else depends on: `.beads` discovery, workspace/repo-path selection, warning suppression in robot mode, JSONL file precedence, BOM handling, malformed-line behavior, model validation, and status/dependency semantics.

Why this matters:
Many operator-visible parity problems are really path or loader mismatches in disguise. This bead should flush out those root causes before they continue leaking into downstream surfaces.

Done means:
There is a concrete list of matching semantics, mismatches, and required fixes/tests.
close_reason: Audit substantially complete with 15+ concrete bugs found and fixed by multiple agents over 5 days. Fixed: git worktree .beads fallback, malformed intermediate .git handling, updated_at>=created_at validation, robot-mode warning suppression, workspace path normalization, relative repo-path discovery ambiguity, correlation feedback workspace routing, sprint metadata workspace routing, historical load workspace awareness, diff/reference workspace resolution, agents-* workspace resolution, workspace dedupe dot-segment handling, disabled repo alias discovery suppression, workspace root discovery, explicit root repo namespacing, historical JSONL filename resolution. Remaining work belongs in bd-2bpl.2.3.1 fix track.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: Progress update: audited loader and path-resolution semantics against legacy worktree expectations and found a real parity gap in .beads discovery for linked git worktrees. Root cause: get_beads_dir() only searched ancestor .beads directories and did not fall back from a worktree .git pointer to the main repo .beads; the first implementation of that fallback also needed canonicalization of commondir to resolve dot-dot segments correctly. Fix landed in src/loader.rs: parse worktree .git, resolve gitdir, canonicalize commondir, derive the main repo root, and use the main-repo .beads when present. Added regression test get_beads_dir_falls_back_to_main_repo_for_git_worktree. Verification: export TMPDIR=/data/tmp && rch exec -- cargo test --lib get_beads_dir_falls_back_to_main_repo_for_git_worktree passed; prior rch exec -- cargo check --all-targets and rch exec -- cargo clippy --all-targets on this slice were green. cargo fmt --check remains red repo-wide due existing unrelated drift in other files, but src/loader.rs was formatted with rustfmt --edition 2024.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: Progress update: hardened the linked-worktree fallback in src/loader.rs after review identified an ancestor-scan correctness hole. Root cause: find_worktree_main_repo_beads_dir_from() used early-return option propagation inside the ancestor loop, so any malformed or non-worktree .git file in an intermediate ancestor aborted the entire scan before reaching the real worktree root. Fix: treat each ancestor independently, continue past invalid .git pointers or unusable commondir data, and only return when a valid main-repo .beads directory is found. Added regression test get_beads_dir_ignores_malformed_intermediate_git_file_during_worktree_fallback alongside the existing worktree fallback tests. Verification: export TMPDIR=/data/tmp && rch exec -- cargo test --lib get_beads_dir_ passed; export TMPDIR=/data/tmp && rch exec -- cargo check --all-targets passed; export TMPDIR=/data/tmp && rch exec -- cargo clippy --all-targets passed with the repo existing warning set. cargo fmt --check still fails on unrelated pre-existing drift in other files outside this slice.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: Progress update: the audit found a model-level validation gap in src/model.rs. Legacy validation requires updated_at to be greater than or equal to created_at when both timestamps are present, but Issue::validate() was not checking that invariant at all. Fix: reject issues whose updated_at is earlier than created_at while still accepting equal timestamps. Added direct unit regressions validate_rejects_updated_at_before_created_at and validate_accepts_equal_created_and_updated_timestamps. Verification: export TMPDIR=/data/tmp && rch exec -- cargo test --lib validate_rejects_updated_at_before_created_at passed; export TMPDIR=/data/tmp && rch exec -- cargo test --lib validate_accepts_equal_created_and_updated_timestamps passed; export TMPDIR=/data/tmp && rch exec -- cargo check --all-targets passed; export TMPDIR=/data/tmp && rch exec -- cargo clippy --all-targets passed with the repo existing warning set. cargo fmt --check still fails only because of unrelated pre-existing formatting drift in other files outside this slice.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: Progress update: found and fixed a CLI-to-loader parity mismatch around warning suppression. Root cause: loader warning suppression only consulted BV_ROBOT=1, but normal bvr --robot-* invocations did not set that environment variable, so malformed-line and invalid-issue warnings could still leak to stderr in ordinary robot mode. Fix: added loader-level robot-warning suppression state, wired main.rs to enable it whenever Cli::is_robot_command() is true before issue loading, and preserved the existing BV_ROBOT env behavior as an additional trigger. Added CLI integration regression robot_triage_suppresses_loader_warnings_without_bv_robot_env to prove malformed JSON warnings stay suppressed during normal --robot-triage usage. Verification: export TMPDIR=/data/tmp && rch exec -- cargo test --test cli_model_validation robot_triage_suppresses_loader_warnings_without_bv_robot_env passed; export TMPDIR=/data/tmp && rch exec -- cargo check --all-targets passed; export TMPDIR=/data/tmp && rch exec -- cargo clippy --all-targets passed with the repo existing warning set. cargo fmt --check still fails only because of unrelated pre-existing drift outside this slice.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: Audit continuation (PearlMill): current loader/model/path code still covers deterministic JSONL selection, first-line BOM stripping, malformed-line suppression in robot mode, workspace discovery/namespacing, linked-worktree .beads fallback, and updated_at >= created_at validation. The `br ready` vs `bvr --robot-next` disagreement reproduced in this session is not a loader/path source-of-truth bug in this repo: `.beads/` currently only contains `issues.jsonl`. The immediate cause is triage-layer behavior in `src/analysis/triage.rs`, which excludes `in_progress` issues from recommendations, while `br ready` still lists the current P0 audit beads despite their `in_progress` status. That belongs under the robot-command audit/fix track (`bd-2bpl.2.2` / `bd-2bpl.2.3.2`), not as a new loader/model/path mismatch. Verification this session: `rch exec -- cargo test --lib get_beads_dir_` passed, `rch exec -- cargo test --lib parse_issues_from_text_strips_bom` passed, and `rch exec -- cargo test --test cli_model_validation diff_since_auto_robot_diff_suppresses_loader_warnings -- --exact` passed.

comments.author: BrightSpire
comments.text: Progress update (BrightSpire): found and fixed a workspace auto-discovery path-normalization bug in src/main.rs. Root cause: workspace_discovery_start_points() mixed a relative --repo-path with an absolute current_dir, so the same .bv/workspace.yaml could be discovered twice under different PathBuf spellings and falsely trip the "workspace auto-discovery is ambiguous" guard. Fix: normalize relative repo_path values against current_dir before assembling discovery start points. Added regression test resolve_issue_load_target_discovers_workspace_from_relative_repo_path_without_ambiguity in the src/main.rs test module, with a CurrentDirGuard to avoid leaking cwd on failure. Verification: export TMPDIR=/data/tmp && rch exec -- cargo test --bin bvr resolve_issue_load_target_discovers_workspace_from_relative_repo_path_without_ambiguity passed; export TMPDIR=/data/tmp && rch exec -- cargo check --all-targets passed; export TMPDIR=/data/tmp && rch exec -- cargo clippy --all-targets passed with existing repo warnings only. export TMPDIR=/data/tmp && rch exec -- cargo fmt --check still fails on unrelated pre-existing formatting drift outside this slice.

comments.author: BrightSpire
comments.text:
Found and fixed another workspace-path mismatch under the loader/path audit.

Problem: the correlation audit commands (`--robot-correlation-stats`, `--robot-explain-correlation`, `--robot-confirm-correlation`, `--robot-reject-correlation`) were still building `.beads/correlation_feedback.jsonl` from raw `--repo-path` / `.`. In workspace mode, that meant a nested repo path would bypass the resolved workspace root and miss workspace-level correlation feedback.

Fix:
- src/main.rs now routes the correlation feedback store through feedback_project_dir(&cli), aligning it with the existing workspace-aware persisted-state behavior.

Regression coverage:
- added cli integration test `correlation_stats_reads_feedback_from_workspace_root_when_repo_path_discovers_workspace` proving `--robot-correlation-stats` reads `workspace_root/.beads/correlation_feedback.jsonl` when `--repo-path` points inside a discovered workspace repo.

Verification:
- `rch exec -- cargo test --test cli_model_validation correlation_stats_reads_feedback_from_workspace_root_when_repo_path_discovers_workspace`

comments.text:
Fresh-eyes follow-up found another workspace-root persisted-state mismatch in sprint metadata flows.

Problem: sprint-bearing CLI surfaces were still loading `sprints.jsonl` from raw `--repo-path` instead of the resolved load-target project dir. That affected `--robot-sprint-list`, `--robot-sprint-show`, forecast sprint resolution, and burndown, allowing workspace-root sprint metadata to be skipped when invoking from a nested repo path under discovered workspace mode.

Fix:
- added `load_sprints_for_cli()` in `src/main.rs`
- routed sprint list/show, forecast sprint lookup, and burndown through the workspace-aware project dir

Regression coverage:

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: Progress update (BrightSpire): found and fixed a stronger historical path-resolution bug. Root cause: load_issues_at_revision() bypassed resolve_issue_load_target() entirely, so --as-of (and the shared git-ref historical load path) ignored --beads-file, explicit/auto-discovered workspace configs, and workspace aggregation semantics; it fell back to a single raw .beads path under repo_path/current_dir. Fix: historical loading now resolves against the actual load target, including per-repo workspace historical loads with the same namespacing/source_repo semantics as live workspace loading. Added regression test load_issues_as_of_uses_workspace_config_instead_of_raw_repo_path in src/main.rs proving --as-of HEAD with --repo-path inside a discovered workspace loads both workspace repos. Verification: export TMPDIR=/data/tmp && rch exec -- cargo test --bin bvr load_issues_as_of_uses_workspace_config_instead_of_raw_repo_path passed; export TMPDIR=/data/tmp && rch exec -- cargo check --all-targets passed; export TMPDIR=/data/tmp && rch exec -- cargo clippy --all-targets passed with the existing repo warning set. export TMPDIR=/data/tmp && rch exec -- cargo fmt --check still fails only because of unrelated pre-existing formatting drift outside this slice.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: Fixed another workspace/history parity gap: --robot-diff --diff-since <git-ref> now routes historical loading and ref resolution through the same workspace-aware load target semantics as live issue loads, so nested --repo-path invocation inside a discovered workspace no longer diffs only the nested repo. Added integration coverage for workspace-root diffing against HEAD~1 from a nested repo path.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: Fresh-eyes follow-up fix: my new workspace-aware diff/reference helper had regressed relative snapshot file resolution by checking only the workspace-promoted project dir. It now tries the workspace-aware project dir first and then falls back to the explicit nested --repo-path when distinct, with a unit regression test covering workspace discovery plus repo-relative snapshots.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: Fixed another workspace/path parity gap: --agents-* early commands were still resolving work_dir from only --workspace or cwd, so nested --repo-path invocation inside an auto-discovered workspace could inspect the nested repo instead of the workspace root. They now use project_dir_for_load_target(), and admin_cli coverage proves --agents-force from a nested repo finds the workspace-root AGENTS.md.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: Picking up the next feasible high-impact lane from bv robot triage. I’m auditing loader/path-resolution semantics in src/loader.rs plus targeted workspace/history integration coverage, looking for another concrete divergence rather than broad speculative review.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: Found and fixed another loader/workspace path-semantics gap in src/loader.rs. Problem: workspace discovery deduped explicit repos by raw path spelling, so an explicit repo path like services/./api could be rediscovered as services/api and loaded twice if prefixes differed. Fix: repo identity keys now canonicalize resolved repo paths before dedupe. Added unit coverage in src/loader.rs (load_workspace_config_dedupes_explicit_repo_paths_with_dot_segments) and E2E coverage in tests/e2e_workspace_history.rs (workspace_discovery_dedupes_explicit_repo_path_aliases). Verification via rch: cargo test --lib loader::tests::load_workspace_config_dedupes_explicit_repo_paths_with_dot_segments -- --exact; cargo test --test e2e_workspace_history workspace_discovery_dedupes_explicit_repo_path_aliases -- --exact; cargo check --all-targets; cargo clippy --all-targets. cargo fmt --check was not rerun this slice; prior repo state already had unrelated formatting drift.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: Taking another concrete loader/path audit slice based on fresh bv robot triage. I’m focusing on src/loader.rs plus workspace-history regressions again to find another real path-resolution or workspace-discovery mismatch without colliding with the active robot/main.rs lane.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: Found and fixed another concrete loader/path semantics bug in src/loader.rs. Problem: workspace configs could list the same repo twice under different path spellings (for example services/api and services/./api) and both explicit entries would load, double-counting one repository under multiple prefixes. Fix: WorkspaceConfig::resolve_repos() now rejects enabled repos whose canonical path identity duplicates an earlier repo entry. Added unit coverage in src/loader.rs: load_workspace_config_rejects_duplicate_repo_path_aliases, plus an end-to-end regression in tests/e2e_workspace_history.rs: workspace_duplicate_repo_alias_returns_error. Verification via rch: cargo test --lib loader::tests::load_workspace_config_rejects_duplicate_repo_path_aliases -- --exact; cargo test --test e2e_workspace_history workspace_duplicate_repo_alias_returns_error -- --exact; cargo check --all-targets; cargo clippy --all-targets. cargo fmt --check still fails from unrelated pre-existing formatting drift outside this slice.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: Hardened the duplicate-repo-alias fix further in src/loader.rs. Problem: the earlier canonical-path dedupe still missed explicit duplicate aliases when the repo directory did not exist yet, because canonicalize() fell back to the raw path spelling and treated services/api and services/./api as different identities. Fix: repo_identity_key() now lexically normalizes fallback paths (skipping . segments and collapsing simple parent/current components) before comparing identities, so duplicate explicit aliases are rejected even before the repo exists. Added unit coverage in src/loader.rs: load_workspace_config_rejects_duplicate_missing_repo_aliases. Verification via rch: cargo test --lib loader::tests::load_workspace_config_rejects_duplicate_missing_repo_aliases -- --exact; cargo check --all-targets; cargo clippy --all-targets. cargo fmt --check still fails from unrelated pre-existing formatting drift outside this slice.

comments.author: ubuntu

I’m fixing that in src/loader.rs with workspace-history regression coverage.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: Found and fixed another concrete loader/discovery semantics bug in src/loader.rs. Problem: disabled explicit repos were still seeding workspace discovery dedupe, so a disabled alias like services/./api could suppress auto-discovery of the real repo path services/api. Fix: discover_workspace_repos() now seeds dedupe only from enabled explicit repos. Added unit coverage in src/loader.rs: load_workspace_config_discovery_ignores_disabled_explicit_repo_aliases and end-to-end coverage in tests/e2e_workspace_history.rs: workspace_discovery_ignores_disabled_explicit_repo_alias. Verification via rch: cargo test --lib loader::tests::load_workspace_config_discovery_ignores_disabled_explicit_repo_aliases -- --exact; cargo test --test e2e_workspace_history workspace_discovery_ignores_disabled_explicit_repo_alias -- --exact; cargo check --all-targets; cargo clippy --all-targets. cargo fmt --check still fails repo-wide on unrelated pre-existing formatting drift outside this slice.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: Found and fixed another concrete loader/discovery semantics bug in src/loader.rs. Problem: discovery had no path representation for the workspace root itself, so a repo rooted at the workspace directory could not be auto-discovered even when the config explicitly asked for discovery.patterns: ['.']. Fix: relative_path_matches_pattern() now recognizes the empty relative path as '.', and discover_workspace_repos() now considers the workspace root as a discovery candidate before walking child directories. Root discoveries are recorded with path '.' and a stable repo name from the workspace root directory name so namespacing remains deterministic. Added unit coverage in src/loader.rs: load_workspace_issues_discovers_workspace_root_repo_when_pattern_matches_dot and end-to-end coverage in tests/e2e_workspace_history.rs: workspace_discovery_can_include_workspace_root_repo. Verification via rch: cargo test --lib loader::tests::load_workspace_issues_discovers_workspace_root_repo_when_pattern_matches_dot -- --exact; cargo test --test e2e_workspace_history workspace_discovery_can_include_workspace_root_repo -- --exact; cargo check --all-targets; cargo clippy --all-targets.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: Found and fixed another concrete loader/path semantics bug in src/loader.rs. Problem: explicit workspace repos pointing at the workspace root itself (for example path: . or path: ./) still derived their fallback repo name from the raw path spelling, which produced bad source_repo / prefix semantics like '.' and '.-'. Fix: resolve_repos() now detects explicit repos that normalize to the workspace root and assigns them the stable workspace-root directory name before namespacing; the existing discovery-root path now uses the same helper. Added unit coverage in src/loader.rs: load_workspace_issues_namespaces_explicit_workspace_root_repo_path and end-to-end coverage in tests/e2e_workspace_history.rs: workspace_explicit_root_repo_path_uses_workspace_name_for_namespacing. Verification via rch: cargo test --lib loader::tests::load_workspace_issues_namespaces_explicit_workspace_root_repo_path -- --exact; cargo test --test e2e_workspace_history workspace_explicit_root_repo_path_uses_workspace_name_for_namespacing -- --exact; cargo check --all-targets; cargo clippy --all-targets.

comments.author: GentleShore
comments.text: Progress update (GentleShore): fixed a historical loader/path-resolution mismatch in src/main.rs. Problem: git-backed historical loads (--as-of and the shared revision loader path) resolved the current JSONL filename before querying the target revision, so repos that changed between .beads/beads.jsonl and .beads/issues.jsonl across commits could fail against the wrong file. Fix: added a revision-aware historical beads-dir resolver that inspects the target git revision, prefers beads.jsonl / issues.jsonl / beads.base.jsonl in historical order, and falls back to the same filtered JSONL scan semantics used live; workspace and repo-path historical loads now use that path, while explicit --beads-file historical loads keep honoring the caller’s exact file. Added regression test as_of_uses_historical_jsonl_filename_when_current_filename_changed in tests/cli_model_validation.rs. Verification via rch: cargo test --test cli_model_validation as_of_uses_historical_jsonl_filename_when_current_filename_changed -- --exact; cargo check --all-targets; cargo clippy --all-targets. cargo fmt --check still reports unrelated pre-existing formatting drift outside this slice.

title: Re-audit each robot command family for behavioral and output-shape equivalence
description:
Purpose:
Review every legacy robot family again with fresh eyes: triage, next, plan, insights, priority, diff, history, forecast, capacity, burndown, suggest, graph, search, label intelligence, recipes/scripts, docs/schema, and admin or operational flags.

Focus:
- Field shape and omission rules.
- Filter semantics and default behavior.
- Path/config resolution.
- Error/warning behavior and deterministic ordering.

Done means:
Every reopened mismatch becomes either a fix bead or a justified divergence note.
close_reason: Audit substantially complete. Multiple agents fixed: RobotNext omission-shape gaps, robot docs/schema/help drift, label-intel payload parity, search usage_hints gap, HookContext relative path resolution, workspace repo path normalization, robot-docs key_fields for 8 flattened commands, workspace diff namespace mismatch, orphans test stats path. E2E coverage expanded from 31 to 39/39 robot commands. 2 pre-existing conformance adversarial fixtures remain (known, not regressions).

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: Audit start (PearlMill): used live `bv --robot-triage`, `bv --robot-plan`, and `bv --robot-next` to pick the next bead. All three identify `bd-2bpl.2.2` / the audit stream as the highest-impact current work. First concrete mismatch already surfaced: live `bv` currently treats the active P0 audit beads (status `in_progress`) as actionable/top-pick candidates and even returns `bd-2bpl.2.2` from `--robot-next`, while the current Rust triage implementation in `src/analysis/triage.rs` filters recommendations with `!issue.is_in_progress()`. That means Rust robot triage/next semantics disagree with the live `bv` prioritization surface for in-progress work. This is squarely a robot-command semantics gap, not a loader/path issue. I am continuing the audit from the reserved robot files and tests; `src/main.rs` is currently reserved by another agent, so I am avoiding edit collisions there until the conflict clears or I have a surgical fix that needs coordination.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text:
Audit continuation (PearlMill): after the `in_progress` triage gap was fixed, I checked the remaining robot-surface audit matrix against live `bv` outputs and the current repo tests. Concrete finding: robot-command coverage is still uneven. Core families such as triage/next/plan/priority/history/diff/forecast/capacity/burndown have visible conformance/e2e presence, but several live robot surfaces appear materially less audited under the current evidence standard: search, label-health, label-flow, label-attention, recipes, and docs/schema.

Live `bv` spot-checks from this session confirm these are real current robot surfaces with nontrivial schemas: `--robot-search` returns top-level keys `[data_hash, dim, generated_at, index, index_path, limit, loaded, mode, provider, query, results, usage_hints]`; `--robot-label-health` returns `[analysis_config, data_hash, generated_at, results, usage_hints]`; `--robot-label-flow` returns `[analysis_config, data_hash, flow, generated_at, usage_hints]`; `--robot-label-attention` returns `[data_hash, generated_at, labels, limit, total_labels, usage_hints]`; `bv --robot-help` also documents `--robot-recipes`, `--robot-docs`, and `--robot-schema`.

Current repo test inventory check: `tests/e2e_robot_matrix.rs` has explicit e2e coverage for docs/schema/metrics but not obvious e2e coverage for search or the label-intel families; `tests/conformance.rs` has broad legacy-core coverage but not obvious command-specific parity assertions for these additive surfaces. Audit implication: these commands should be treated as either (a) additive/divergent-by-design robot surfaces that need truthful documentation, or (b) surfaces needing stronger regression coverage under `bd-2bpl.2.4.1`, rather than being silently assumed covered at the same standard as the legacy-core families.

Generated single-command schemas for several versioned outputs omitted output_format/version even though the live payloads include them. Fixed in src/robot.rs by introducing a versioned schema-property helper and applying it to versioned command schemas (search, recipes, label-health/flow/attention, metrics, sprint-list/show, correlation-stats, orphans, file-beads/hotspots/impact/relations/related/blocker-chain/impact-network/causality/drift). comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: Audit findings (StormyTower): Identified 2 remaining workspace path resolution bugs in failing integration tests. (1) robot_diff_snapshot_prefers_workspace_relative_file_over_caller_cwd_shadow: snapshot resolution finds 2 new issues instead of 1, suggesting workspace-root snapshot is not being preferred over cwd-relative. (2) robot_orphans_uses_workspace_root_history_when_repo_path_discovers_workspace: workspace-root git history not visible to robot-orphans when --repo-path discovers workspace. Both are in the workspace/diff/orphan resolution path in src/main.rs and relate to project_dir_for_load_target behavior. These belong under bd-2bpl.2.3.2 (robot command fixes) rather than the audit bead itself.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: Root cause analysis (StormyTower): The robot_diff_snapshot_prefers_workspace_relative_file_over_caller_cwd_shadow failure is caused by namespace mismatch between diff baseline and current issues. In workspace mode, current issues get namespace-prefixed (e.g. api-BD-1) via load_workspace_issues_with_summary, but the diff baseline loaded via load_issues_for_diff uses load_issues_from_file which returns raw unprefixed issues. The diff engine then sees 0 matching IDs and reports all current issues as new. Fix needs to either apply workspace namespacing to diff baselines when the load target is a workspace, or ensure snapshot files contain pre-namespaced issues. This belongs under bd-2bpl.2.3.2.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: Progress update (StormyTower): Fixed the workspace diff namespace mismatch bug. Root cause: file-based diff baselines loaded via --diff-since had raw unprefixed issue IDs while workspace-loaded current issues were namespace-prefixed, causing all issues to appear as new. Fix: load_issues_for_diff now applies single-repo workspace namespace prefixing to baseline issues when the load target is a WorkspaceConfig. The robot_diff_snapshot_prefers_workspace_relative_file test now passes. The robot_orphans_uses_workspace_root_history failure remains pre-existing (separate workspace-history resolution bug).

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: Progress update (StormyTower): Fixed the robot_orphans_uses_workspace_root_history test. Root cause was NOT a workspace path resolution bug as previously suspected - the test was simply checking json[total_commits] instead of json[stats][total_commits]. The OrphanReport struct has stats: OrphanStats (not flattened), so total_commits lives under stats. The workspace discovery and git history resolution were working correctly all along. All 55 cli_model_validation + e2e_robot_matrix + schema_validation tests now pass (0 failures). Only 2 pre-existing conformance adversarial fixture mismatches remain.

title: Close reopened robot/CLI mismatches discovered by the fresh audit
description:
Purpose:
This is the implementation bucket for concrete robot/CLI bugs exposed by the fresh audit. Do not close it based on planning alone. Populate it via child comments or follow-up beads that reference the exact mismatch and root cause.

Acceptance:
- Every fix includes targeted regression coverage.
- New path/config behaviors are covered from the CLI entrypoint, not only internal helpers.
- The final state preserves determinism and legacy-compatible defaults unless a divergence is explicitly documented.
close_reason: Both child fix beads (bd-2bpl.2.3.1 loader fixes, bd-2bpl.2.3.2 robot fixes) are closed. All audit-surfaced mismatches were fixed inline during the audits with regression tests. Verified: 62/62 e2e robot, 61/61 schema, 73/75 conformance (2 known adversarial), 66 loader, 28 model, 34 workspace tests all pass.

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text:
Planning refinement:
This is now a coordination container. Actual implementation should usually land on `bd-2bpl.2.3.1` and/or `bd-2bpl.2.3.2`, with each code change tied back to a specific audited mismatch.

title: Fix loader/model/path-resolution mismatches surfaced by the robot audit
description:
Purpose:
Implement concrete fixes for loader, model, and path-resolution mismatches discovered in `bd-2bpl.2.1`. Keep this bead focused on discovery/root-cause classes that affect all downstream robot workflows, not on per-command payload details.

Required scope:
- `.beads` and workspace discovery semantics.
- `--repo-path`, `--workspace`, and `--beads-file` precedence and project-root inference.
- JSONL precedence, BOM/malformed-line handling, warning suppression, and validation semantics.
- Model/status/dependency interpretation differences that can change graph shape or command output.

Acceptance:
- Root-cause fixes are implemented without reducing existing functionality.
- Regressions are covered with targeted unit and CLI-entry integration tests.
- Any intentional divergence is recorded explicitly in comments or follow-up beads.
close_reason: All fixes were implemented inline during the bd-2bpl.2.1 audit by prior agents: 15+ loader/model/path bugs fixed with regression tests. Verified: 66 loader tests, 28 model tests, 34 workspace history tests all pass. 54/55 CLI model validation tests pass (1 flaky drift test is environment-dependent, passes in isolation). No remaining unfixed mismatches in scope.

title: Fix robot command/output/filter/default mismatches surfaced by the fresh audit
description:
Purpose:
Implement concrete robot-mode fixes from `bd-2bpl.2.2`: payload shape, omission rules, sorting/determinism, filter semantics, default behaviors, and operational/admin command compatibility.

Acceptance:
- Every landed change references a specific audited mismatch and root cause.
- Fixes preserve deterministic output and legacy-compatible defaults unless a divergence is explicitly approved and documented.
- Existing features remain available; no simplification that drops capability is allowed.
close_reason: All fixes from bd-2bpl.2.2 audit were implemented inline by prior agents: RobotNext omission-shape, docs/schema/help drift, label-intel payload parity, search usage_hints, HookContext paths, workspace paths, robot-docs key_fields for 8 commands, workspace diff namespacing, orphans stats path. Verified: 62/62 e2e_robot_matrix, 61/61 schema_validation, 73/75 conformance (2 known adversarial fixture mismatches are pre-existing, documented in audit). E2E coverage expanded from 31 to 39/39 robot commands.

title: Expand conformance and integration harnesses for any reopened robot gaps
description:
Purpose:
Once gaps are identified, the harness must prove them closed. This bead covers adding or tightening fixture-backed conformance, schema validation, and integration tests so the same categories of mismatch do not regress.

Done means:
The evidence burden for robot parity is encoded in tests, not tribal memory.
design:
Testing ladder:
1. Module or helper-level unit tests for root-cause semantics.
2. Conformance fixtures or schema checks where legacy output shape is the source of truth.
3. Binary/integration coverage from the real CLI entrypoint.
4. When end-to-end operator workflow matters, shell/e2e scripts with detailed stdout/stderr and replay logging.

Do not close this bead on narrow unit coverage alone.
acceptance_criteria:
- Reopened robot gaps are covered by the full appropriate testing ladder, not a single narrow test layer.
- Unit, conformance, integration, and e2e/logging obligations are satisfied where relevant.
- Failure diagnostics are good enough that a future agent can localize the regression quickly.
close_reason: Both children closed: 2.4.1 (robot regressions verified) and 2.4.2 (robot CLI e2e scripts by SageSpring). Coverage: 62 e2e robot tests, 61 schema validation, shell harness with artifact bundles.

title: Add robot unit and conformance regressions for all reopened parity gaps
description:
Purpose:
Turn reopened robot mismatches into durable proof. Add module-level unit tests, fixture-backed conformance, schema validations, and focused binary tests as needed.

Logging requirement:
Failure output must clearly identify the command family, fixture, and shape/semantic dimension that regressed so debugging does not require guesswork.

Acceptance:
- Every reopened robot gap has at least one durable regression test.
- Conformance fixtures are updated or added when legacy output behavior is the source of truth.
- Test names and assertions make the user-visible contract obvious.
close_reason: All reopened robot/loader gaps have durable regression tests added inline during audits. Verified: 3 get_beads_dir tests (worktree fallback, malformed .git), 9 validate_rejects tests, 2 validate_accepts tests, 62/62 e2e_robot_matrix, 61/61 schema_validation, 34 workspace_history, 54 cli_model_validation. Coverage includes: loader discovery, model validation, workspace path normalization, robot payload shapes, warning suppression, diff namespace, sprint/baseline/feedback workspace routing, historical load awareness, admin workspace resolution.

title: Add robot CLI integration and e2e scripts with detailed diagnostics and artifact capture
description:
Purpose:
Cover whole-command workflows that unit and fixture tests cannot fully validate. Add CLI integration coverage and, where useful, shell/e2e scripts that capture stdout, stderr, replay commands, and fixture context for failures.

Acceptance:
- Reopened robot workflows are exercised from the real CLI entrypoint.
- Diagnostic output is rich enough for a future agent to reproduce failures quickly.
- Scripts and tests remain deterministic and `rch`-friendly.
close_reason: Added a dedicated shell-level robot CLI e2e harness plus richer Rust e2e replay artifacts. Robot artifact bundles now snapshot fixtures, record binary/cwd/stdout/stderr metadata, and emit direct-binary replay scripts instead of cargo-run replays. Verified with ./scripts/e2e_robot_cli.sh /home/ubuntu/.local/bin/bvr, export TMPDIR=/data/tmp && rch exec -- cargo fmt --check, export TMPDIR=/data/tmp && rch exec -- cargo check --all-targets, export TMPDIR=/data/tmp && rch exec -- cargo clippy --all-targets, and export TMPDIR=/data/tmp && rch exec -- cargo test --test e2e_robot_matrix.

title: Audit and close interactive TUI workflow gaps without chasing pixel parity
description:
Background:
The TUI does not need to look exactly like the legacy Go implementation, but it does need to support the same operator workflows, navigation loops, search/discovery behaviors, and task-completion outcomes.

Goal:
Audit core TUI workflows with fresh eyes, identify interaction gaps or regressions, and improve the frankentui implementation until it covers the legacy workflow surface and is at least as usable.

Acceptance:
- Main, board, insights, graph, and history workflows are audited end-to-end.
- Missing or weaker interactions are converted into concrete implementation tasks.
- The resulting TUI is behaviorally equivalent or better for core legacy jobs, even if visual styling differs.
close_reason: TUI workflow gaps closed. Audit track (3.1-3.3) complete. Fix track (3.4) complete. Test track (3.5) complete. Redesign track (3.6) complete.

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text:
TUI track guidance:
Judge the Rust TUI by workflow coverage, not by pixel matching. A different layout or nicer presentation is acceptable if operators can still complete the same tasks at least as effectively. Prioritize discovery, navigation, search/filter loops, detail usefulness, and mode transitions.

title: Audit main-view operator workflows and detail-pane usefulness against legacy jobs-to-be-done
description:
Purpose:
Audit the default TUI surface as an operator tool rather than as a rendering artifact. The question is whether a user can browse, prioritize, inspect blockers, search, sort, and decide what to do next as effectively as in legacy `bv`.

Focus:
Selection behavior, detail richness, search loops, filter transitions, sort order expectations, focus changes, and empty/no-hit states.

Outcome:
Produce a concrete list of workflow gaps and fixes, not aesthetic opinions.
close_reason: Audit complete. 18 gaps documented (5 critical, 8 significant, 5 minor). Key findings: no Main detail scroll, no granular status filters, no combination filters, search scope limited, list rows too sparse. Detail pane is strong. Full findings in comment for bd-2bpl.3.4.1 to consume.

comments.author: FuchsiaAnchor
comments.text:
## Main-View Operator Workflow Audit — FuchsiaAnchor (2026-03-19)

### Methodology
Thorough code-level audit of src/tui.rs Main mode: list rendering, detail pane, search, filters, sort, selection, focus, empty states, key handlers, and mode transitions. Compared against what a triage TUI needs to support operator workflows (browse, prioritize, inspect blockers, search, decide next).

### CRITICAL GAPS (workflow-blocking)

**G1. Detail pane not scrollable in Main mode.** Board mode has Ctrl+j/k detail scroll, but Main mode does not. Long issues with descriptions, dependencies, and comments are truncated. Operators can't read the full detail without switching to Board mode. (src/tui.rs: board_detail_scroll_offset exists, no equivalent for main)

**G2. No granular status filters.** Only 4 filter states: All/Open/Closed/Ready. Can't filter to 'in_progress' or 'blocked' specifically. Ready is useful but operators doing triage need to isolate blocked items. (src/tui.rs:4403-4413 set_list_filter)

**G3. No combination filters.** Only one label + one repo + one status filter at a time. Can't do 'p0 + ready + repo:X'. (src/tui.rs:4403-4408)

**G4. Search doesn't cover description/notes.** Search matches ID, title, status, type, labels only. Missing description, notes, design, acceptance criteria — the text fields operators actually grep for. (src/tui.rs:4916-4934 main_search_matches)

**G5. List rows lack visual density for scanning.** Format is '{marker} {id:<14} {status:<11} p{prio} {title}'. No type icon, no label chips, no blocker indicator, no assignee hint, no last-updated. At scale, operators can't distinguish ready vs blocked vs assigned items without opening each one. (src/tui.rs:5596-5604)

### SIGNIFICANT GAPS (operator friction)

**G6. No column headers in list.** Fields are unlabeled. New users can't tell what each column means.

**G7. No position counter.** No '3/15' or scroll position indicator. On large backlogs, operators lose spatial context.

**G8. Sort direction not indicated.** 'priority' sorts ascending (0=highest) which is correct but non-obvious. No arrow or asc/desc label shown. Only 5 sort modes with no direction toggle — must cycle through all to get the reverse. (src/tui.rs:4415-4420 cycle_list_sort)

**G9. No Shift+Tab (reverse focus cycle).** Tab cycles List->Detail->List but overshooting requires full loop. (src/tui.rs:2699-2727)

**G10. Selection marker is weak.** Just a '>' character at column 0. No highlight, no background color, no bold. Easy to lose on dense screens.

**G11. Page Up/Down jump fixed 10 rows.** Not terminal-height-aware. On tall terminals, 10 rows is too few; on short ones, it jumps past the viewport.

**G12. No 'go to issue by ID' shortcut.** Must use search (/) to navigate to a specific issue. A direct 'g' then type ID would be faster for known targets.

**G13. Empty states give no guidance.** '(no issues match filter: open)' doesn't suggest pressing 'a' to clear filters or '/' to search. Detail pane says 'No issues to display' without mentioning how to load data.

### MINOR GAPS (polish)

**G14. Filter state not fully visible.** Active label/repo filters shown in header as +label:X but easy to miss. No combined filter summary.

**G15. Focus state indicator subtle.** Header shows 'focus=detail' in small text. No visual pane border change or highlight for focused pane.

**G16. Mode transitions reset state.** Switching Main->Board->Main resets board grouping. Insights panel selection, graph node selection lost on mode switch.

**G17. Search clears on mode change.** If operator has active search in Main, switches to Insights to check something, comes back — search is gone.

**G18. No 'next blocked' or 'next ready' shortcut.** Triage operators would benefit from jumping directly between blocked/ready items.

### STRONG POINTS (no gaps)

- Detail pane is exceptionally comprehensive: dependencies (upstream/open gate/downstream), graph metrics (PageRank, betweenness, eigenvector, HITS, k-core, slack, critical depth), triage snapshot, comments, history
- OSC-8 hyperlinks for external refs with keyboard actions (o open, y copy)
- Self-describing affordance hints in detail (C copy id, L label filter, w repo filter, t time-travel)
- Search wrapping (n/N cycles) with match position counter
- Filter transitions reset scroll and adjust selection properly
- Priority hints toggle (p) shows triage score breakdown with component-level detail

### RECOMMENDATIONS FOR bd-2bpl.3.4.1 (fix bead)

Priority order for fixes:
1. G1 (detail scroll) — most impactful, prevents reading long issues
2. G5 (list density) — second most impactful, affects every scan
3. G2+G3 (filter gaps) — third most impactful, limits triage workflows
4. G4 (search scope) — frequently needed
5. G7+G10 (position/selection) — orientation aids

title: Audit board, insights, and graph modes for workflow completeness and navigation parity
description:
Purpose:
The secondary visual modes need to support the same discovery and decision-making loops as legacy `bv`. Audit lane traversal, inline detail use, metric explanation flows, dependency navigation, search behavior, and breakpoint/layout transitions.

Done means:
We know exactly where the Rust TUI is weaker, equivalent, or already better for these modes.
close_reason: Audit completed with code/test/render evidence. Recorded concrete weaker/equivalent findings for board, insights, and graph modes; unblocked bd-2bpl.3.4.2 plus redesign epics bd-2bpl.3.6.7/.8/.9 with specific workflow gaps instead of aesthetic-only notes.

comments.author: GoldenHill
comments.text:
Audit evidence (2026-03-18, GoldenHill):

Evidence reviewed:
- docs: AGENTS.md, README.md, FEATURE_PARITY.md
- implementation: src/tui.rs board/insights/graph state, selection, search, detail, and mode-toggle paths
- proof: inline unit tests around board/insights/graph behavior plus live debug renders via `bvr --debug-render board|insights|graph` at 120x40 and board at 72x30

Findings:
1. Board mode is functionally stronger than a placeholder: grouping (`s`), empty-lane toggle (`e`), within-lane nav (`j/k`), lane jumps (`h/l`, `H/L`, `1-4`, `0/$`), search, and detail dependency navigation all exist and are well regression-tested. This is not a missing-capability problem anymore.
2. Board mode is still weaker than a credible kanban workspace at narrow/medium widths. The live 72-column render collapses into truncated grouped text plus one sidecar card, so lane-level signal and spatial memory remain weak. This confirms the current FEATURE_PARITY.md `partial` status and feeds directly into `bd-2bpl.3.6.7` / `bd-2bpl.3.4.2`.
3. Insights mode has a real workflow mismatch: the list pane can show bottleneck-ranked/open-work rows while the detail pane remains pinned to an unrelated globally-selected issue. In the live debug render, the bottleneck list starts with open redesign beads while detail stays on closed `bd-0yfi`. Root cause appears to be that the list is panel-ranked text but `selected_issue()` still follows the generic visible-issue selection path rather than the current insights panel ordering. This is a concrete parity gap for `bd-2bpl.3.4.2` / `bd-2bpl.3.6.9`.
4. Graph mode has a similar context mismatch: the detail pane can focus a node far down the graph-ranked order (`Focus: node 159/272 -> bd-0yfi`) while the visible list viewport starts elsewhere, making the mode feel like a detached metric report instead of a coherent topology explorer. The likely source is the split between `graph_visible_issue_indices()` ordering and generic selection/detail resolution. This is a concrete parity gap for `bd-2bpl.3.4.2` / `bd-2bpl.3.6.8`.
5. Search and keybinding coverage are not the limiting factor for these three modes. The codebase already has strong regression proof for panel cycling, search, lane jumps, graph traversal, heatmap drill, and mode toggles. The remaining gaps are workflow/context/product-shape issues, not absence of commands.

Conclusion:
- Board: implemented and test-covered, but still visually/report-structured rather than workspace-native.
- Insights: analytically useful, but current list/detail selection model can desynchronize and weaken operator trust.
- Graph: metrics/traversal exist, but list/detail context can desynchronize and the mode still reads as report-native.

This audit is sufficient to unblock `bd-2bpl.3.4.2` and the redesign epics with concrete targets rather than aesthetic opinions.

title: Audit history mode and cross-mode navigation loops against real operator tasks
description:
Purpose:
History mode is easy to overclaim because individual features can exist without the whole operator workflow feeling complete. Audit commit/bead switching, file tree interaction, search modes, confidence filters, jump-back behavior, and cross-mode transitions.

Acceptance:
The bead documents whether history mode truly covers the legacy investigative workflows or just approximates them.
close_reason: Audit complete. 13 gaps documented (5 critical, 5 significant, 3 minor). Key findings: no bidirectional bead-commit navigation, no field-level change tracking, no diff viewing, cross-mode state lost on jumps, opaque confidence scoring. Strong points: dual-view switching, confidence filtering, multi-mode search, lifecycle milestones. Full findings in comment for bd-2bpl.3.4.3.

comments.author: FuchsiaAnchor
comments.text:
## History Mode & Cross-Mode Navigation Audit — FuchsiaAnchor (2026-03-19)

### Methodology
Code-level audit of src/tui.rs History mode, analysis/history.rs, and analysis/git_history.rs. Evaluated bead/git switching, file tree, search modes, confidence filters, cross-mode jumps, and overall investigative workflow completeness.

### WHAT WORKS WELL
- Dual-view investigation: Bead ↔ Git mode toggle (v key) with shared filters
- Confidence filtering: 4-step threshold (0/50/75/90%) via c key, always visible in header
- File tree filtering: f key toggles, Enter on file filters commits touching that path
