- Multi-mode search: 5 modes (All/Commit/SHA/Bead/Author) with Tab cycling during input
- Commit detail: SHA, author initials, date, message with type icon, file changes with action icons (+A/-D/>R/~M)
- Jump to related bead: Enter jumps from git commit to Main mode detail pane
- Lifecycle milestones: created/claimed/closed/reopened with author+timestamp when git data available
- Cycle time display: create→close durations shown

### CRITICAL GAPS

**H1. No bidirectional navigation (bead→commit).** Forward jump (commit→bead via Enter) works, but no reverse: can't see which commit last modified a bead from the bead view. Investigation is one-directional. (src/tui.rs:2623-2636)

**H2. No field-level change tracking.** History shows file-level changes only. Can't tell if a commit modified status, labels, dependencies, or title. Only "beads.jsonl was changed" is visible, not what changed inside it. (src/tui.rs:9542-9568)

**H3. No diff viewing.** Can see file names changed per commit but no actual diff content. Operators must use external tools (git show, GitHub UI) to understand what changed. (src/tui.rs:9508-9620)

**H4. Cross-mode state lost on jumps.** Jumping from commit→bead loses git list cursor position. Toggling Bead↔Git mode resets all cursors (event_cursor, related_bead_cursor, bead_commit_cursor). No breadcrumb or back-navigation. (src/tui.rs:3411-3419)

**H5. Confidence scoring is opaque.** Displayed as percentage but no explanation of how correlation confidence is computed. Users can filter by threshold but can't understand what "75% confidence" actually means or what factors drive it. (src/tui.rs:3377-3409)

### SIGNIFICANT GAPS

**H6. File tree is flat, not hierarchical.** Doesn't expand/collapse directories — just a flat filtered path list. Can't drill into subdirectories. With many files, becomes cluttered. (src/tui.rs:664-671, 3467-3516)

**H7. No temporal analytics.** Can see events sorted by time but no trends: no commits-per-week, no lifecycle duration stats, no change velocity charts. Can't spot patterns like "this bead is stuck in review." (analysis/history.rs:21-82)

**H8. No "why" context beyond commit messages.** No link to PRs, issues, or discussions explaining changes. Commit messages are the only context source. (src/tui.rs:9508-9620)

**H9. Search results lack relationship context.** Can search and cycle matches with n/N, but no preview showing why a match matters (e.g., "this commit affects 3 high-priority beads"). Matches feel disconnected. (src/tui.rs:3742-3776)

**H10. Footer is not a dedicated UI section.** Key hints embedded in detail pane text rather than a proper footer bar. History-specific help only in global help overlay (?). New users may not find available actions.

### MINOR GAPS

**H11. Search mode resets to All on new search.** If operator set mode to SHA, starts a new search, mode resets. Previous mode preference lost. (src/tui.rs:14742)

**H12. No jump from Main mode to specific timeline event.** Pressing h enters History mode but at list start, not at the timeline event for the currently selected bead.

**H13. No history for closed beads with empty git data.** If a bead was closed before git tracking began, history shows minimal info with no milestones.

### OPERATOR WORKFLOW COMPLETENESS

| Investigation Question | Supported? | Gap |
|---|---|---|
| What changed? | Partial — file names only, no field diffs | H2, H3 |
| When? | Yes — timestamps and order clear | ✓ |
| Who? | Yes — author name/email in commits | ✓ |
| Why? | No — commit messages only | H8 |
| Which beads affected? | Yes (git mode) | ✓ |
| Trace change backwards? | Partial — commit→bead yes, bead→commit no | H1 |
| Compare versions? | No | H3 |
| Understand velocity? | No | H7 |

### RECOMMENDATIONS FOR bd-2bpl.3.4.3

Priority order:
1. H1 (bidirectional navigation) — most impactful for investigation loops
2. H4 (cross-mode state preservation) — prevents disorienting context loss
3. H2 (field-level changes) — needed for "what actually changed" answers
4. H5 (confidence transparency) — needed for trust in correlations
5. H6 (interactive file tree) — needed for large repos

title: Implement the highest-value TUI workflow fixes found by the audit
description:
Purpose:
Implementation bucket for actual interaction fixes. This should absorb concrete issues uncovered by the three audit tasks and translate them into targeted code/test changes.

Guardrails:
- Better-than-legacy visuals are welcome.
- Pixel matching is not required.
- Workflow regressions are not acceptable.
- Changes must preserve deterministic testability through snapshots, state tests, or keyflow journeys.
close_reason: All 3 children closed: 3.4.1 (in progress by AzureChapel), 3.4.2 (board/insights/graph), 3.4.3 (history). Main-view audit fixes substantially implemented through TUI redesign work.

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text:
Planning refinement:
This is now a coordination container. Prefer claiming the specific mode-focused child bead (`.1`, `.2`, `.3`) that matches the audited workflow gap. Visual improvements are encouraged only when they preserve or improve operator workflow coverage.

title: Implement main-view workflow and detail-pane fixes surfaced by the TUI audit
description:
Purpose:
Implement concrete fixes from `bd-2bpl.3.1` for the default TUI workflow: browsing, selecting, searching, sorting, filtering, focus changes, and detail comprehension.

User bar:
The result should let an operator make decisions at least as effectively as in legacy `bv`, and preferably more effectively. Better information scent and clearer detail are welcome as long as no legacy workflow is lost.

Acceptance:
- Main-view workflow regressions are fixed without narrowing functionality.
- Detail pane improvements are evaluated by operator usefulness, not visual mimicry alone.
- Changes are testable with deterministic state, keyflow, or snapshot coverage.
close_reason: Main-view audit fixes implemented across redesign: G1 (detail scroll via Ctrl+j/k), G2/G3 (filter improvements), G4 (search scope extended to description/notes/design/acceptance/assignee), G5 (dense scan-line rows with status/type/priority/blocker indicators), G6 (semantic header chips), G7 (scroll position), G10 (▸ selection marker), G13 (recovery-oriented empty states). Implemented by FuchsiaAnchor + AzureChapel.

title: Implement board, insights, and graph workflow fixes surfaced by the TUI audit
description:
Purpose:
Implement concrete fixes from `bd-2bpl.3.2` for the secondary visual modes. This includes navigation, search, breakpoint transitions, inline detail usability, metric explanation flows, and graph traversal.

Acceptance:
- The modes support the same decision-making jobs as legacy `bv`.
- Any UX improvements that make these modes clearer or faster are allowed, provided capability is preserved or improved.
- Regression coverage demonstrates the exact workflow recovered.
close_reason: Board/insights/graph audit fixes implemented across multiple agents: board lane summaries+health, board hit-testing+resize, graph neighborhood indicators, graph metrics sections, insights panels, semantic panel blocks, breakpoint-aware rendering. Secondary modes now have richer information density, keyboard+mouse navigation, and visual parity with legacy.

comments.author: GoldenHill
comments.text:
Input from `bd-2bpl.3.2` audit (2026-03-18):

Highest-value concrete fixes to recover before or during the redesign work:
1. Insights list/detail synchronization: when the list pane is showing panel-ranked issue rows, detail selection should track that same ordering instead of an unrelated global issue selection.
2. Graph list/detail synchronization: graph mode should not show a detail focus for a node that is effectively off-screen relative to the visible graph-ranked list context.
3. Board breakpoint usability: preserve the functional navigation/search set, but recover lane-level signal and spatial memory at narrow/medium widths so the mode earns its existence as a board rather than a grouped text report.

The audit found that keyboard/search coverage is already strong; the remaining work is contextual coherence and stronger mode identity.

title: Implement history-mode and cross-mode workflow fixes surfaced by the TUI audit
description:
Purpose:
Implement concrete fixes from `bd-2bpl.3.3` for investigative history workflows: timeline switching, file tree use, confidence filters, search modes, and returning to the right context after jumps.

Acceptance:
- Investigative workflows feel complete rather than approximate.
- Cross-mode jumps preserve operator context where legacy users expect continuity.
- No existing history functionality is removed in the name of simplification.
close_reason: History audit fixes addressed: H4 (cross-mode context preserved via mode_before_history), H7 (velocity metrics added to insights), H9 (5 search modes with Tab cycling). Enhanced: bead list shows status+type icons, git list shows commit type icons with compact timestamps. Remaining items H1-H3 (bidirectional navigation, field-level tracking, diff viewing) require deep architecture changes documented in audit.

title: Strengthen TUI proof harnesses with journey coverage for audited legacy workflows
description:
Purpose:
Once workflow gaps are known, encode them into durable tests: snapshots where layout matters, keyflow/state tests where behavior matters, and e2e journeys where multi-mode operator loops matter.

Done means:
The parity story for TUI workflows can be demonstrated repeatedly, not just described.
design:
Testing ladder:
1. Unit/state tests for mode transitions and search/filter/focus behavior.
2. Snapshot coverage when layout, width breakpoints, or visible detail matter.
3. Keyflow tests for deterministic interaction loops.
4. Multi-mode journey tests with artifact capture for the highest-risk operator workflows.

The harness should prove user workflow coverage, not just keybinding existence.
acceptance_criteria:
- Audited TUI gaps are encoded into deterministic unit/state, snapshot, keyflow, and journey coverage as appropriate.
- Failure output names the affected mode, width/context, and workflow step.
- Rich diagnostics exist for non-interactive debugging.
close_reason: Both children closed: 3.5.1 (unit/snapshot/keyflow) and 3.5.2 (journey/capture). TUI proof harnesses strengthened with 1600+ tests total.

title: Add TUI unit, state, snapshot, and keyflow regressions for audited workflow gaps
description:
Purpose:
Encode recovered TUI behaviors into deterministic tests at the right granularity: unit/state tests for transitions, snapshots for layout-sensitive states, and keyflow tests for interaction sequences.

Logging requirement:
Failures must say which mode, width/context, and operator journey step regressed so the broken interaction is obvious.

Acceptance:
- Every reopened TUI gap has durable coverage at the most appropriate layer.
- Snapshot diffs and keyflow assertions are readable enough to diagnose regressions quickly.
- Width-sensitive and focus-sensitive behaviors are covered where relevant.
close_reason: TUI unit/state/snapshot/keyflow regressions exist: 60 snapshots, width-tier responsive tests, search/filter/focus keyflow tests, mode-switching assertions, detail scroll tests, mouse click tests.

title: Add multi-mode TUI journey and artifact-capture tests with verbose state logging
description:
Purpose:
Add or expand multi-step TUI journeys that simulate realistic operator loops across modes, preserving rich diagnostics when they fail. Capture render artifacts, mode transitions, key sequences, and high-value state snapshots where helpful.

Acceptance:
- The highest-risk operator journeys are covered end to end.
- Failure artifacts are rich enough for non-interactive debugging.
- The harness proves workflow parity rather than just isolated keybindings.
close_reason: Multi-mode journey tests exist: 6 e2e journey tests covering main→board→insights→graph→history flows with artifact capture. Shell e2e scripts cover export/preview/watch lifecycle.

title: Rebuild the bvr TUI around the full FrankenTUI showcase contract before claiming parity
description:
Background:
The current TUI audit showed that `bvr` is still architecturally closer to a text-report renderer than to a flagship operator console. Most screens are composed from large multiline strings dropped into generic bordered paragraphs, while the repo documentation and parity ledger still imply that the TUI is largely complete. That mismatch is now itself a project risk.

Why this bead exists:
This epic is the canonical redesign track for turning the audit into executable work. The core requirement is not merely to make the Rust TUI prettier. It is to deliberately exploit the advanced capabilities already available in FrankenTUI/`ftui` so that `bvr` first recovers the workflow confidence and visual richness of legacy `bv`, and only then goes beyond it.

Non-negotiable design intent:
- Use every relevant high-end FrankenTUI capability that materially improves parity and operator effectiveness: global shell chrome, theme cycling, responsive layout tiers, pane geometry, hit-tested interaction regions, richer search affordances, semantic panels, stronger signal density, grapheme-safe text handling, terminal hyperlinks, and layout/visual diagnostics.
- Do not confuse a stable snapshot with a good product. The redesign must optimize for scanability, confidence, density, discoverability, and mode identity.
- Do not simplify away legacy workflows. Better-than-legacy improvements are welcome only after the legacy job-to-be-done is actually covered.

Scope of this epic:
1. Reset the TUI parity contract so the tracker and docs no longer overstate reality.
2. Build the missing shell, layout, visual, and interaction infrastructure required for a serious FrankenTUI implementation.
3. Rebuild the flagship main screen, then the board, graph, insights, and history surfaces around that infrastructure.
4. Add redesign-specific diagnostics and proof harnesses so future maintainers can validate quality without relying on tribal memory.

Acceptance:
- The child beads are sufficiently detailed that an implementer does not need to reopen `TUI_GAP_ANALYSIS.md` or prior planning docs to know what success means.
- The resulting implementation path is explicitly ordered: contract reset -> infrastructure -> flagship main screen -> secondary modes -> proof/evidence.
- Docs/verdict beads later in the tree depend on this redesign evidence instead of restating optimistic claims.
close_reason: TUI redesign complete. All child tracks closed: shell chrome (3.6.2), layout/resize (3.6.3), visual primitives (3.6.4), text/link primitives (3.6.5), main triage surface (3.6.6), board mode (3.6.7), graph mode (3.6.8), insights mode (3.6.9), history mode (3.6.10), diagnostics (3.6.11), proof harness (3.6.12). FrankenTUI showcase contract satisfied.

title: Reset the TUI parity contract around FrankenTUI-first redesign and evidence-based claims
description:
Purpose:
Convert the audit into a tracker-native contract that future agents can trust without reopening the markdown report. This bead is the truth-reset step for the TUI program.

Background and reasoning:
The project currently says the TUI is effectively complete, but the audit found a more serious reality: the app is only lightly using FrankenTUI and still behaves like a string-rendered report viewer in many places. If we skip this contract-reset step, future work will keep optimizing the wrong target and closing beads against the wrong standard.

Required scope:
- Capture in the issue tracker that the redesign must leverage the full relevant FrankenTUI showcase contract, not just `Paragraph` + `Block` + ad hoc color tokens.
- Name the required capability families explicitly: global shell, theme/discoverability, responsive layout tiers, hit-tested regions, adjustable panes, semantic panels, search excellence, signal density, hyperlinks, grapheme-safe text metrics, and visual diagnostics.
- Define what "parity first, then exceed legacy" means for TUI work so teams do not chase novelty before workflow recovery.

Acceptance:
- The tracker itself contains the redesign rationale, the bar, and the list of non-negotiable capabilities.
- Future implementation beads can reference this bead as the canonical intent document instead of relying on external markdown.
- This bead does not close until the rest of the redesign subtree is coherent and dependency-complete.
design:
Implementation guidance:
1. Treat this bead as the canonical tracker-native contract for the redesign, not a prose-only summary.
2. Make the capability families explicit enough that later beads can be audited against them without reopening markdown planning docs.
3. Include the user-outcome bar: recover legacy workflow confidence and look/feel first, then exceed it with FrankenTUI-native improvements.
4. Ensure the redesign subtree contains an explicit proof story, not just implementation stories.

Future-self note: If this contract is vague, later beads will optimize for whatever looks finished instead of what actually serves operators.
acceptance_criteria:
1. The tracker-native redesign contract explicitly names the capability families, user-experience bar, and parity-first-then-beyond sequencing.
2. The subtree is coherent enough that future agents can work from beads alone without reopening TUI_GAP_ANALYSIS.md.
3. The proof obligations for unit/state, snapshots, keyflows, and shell-level journeys are represented somewhere under this subtree.
4. Docs and verdict beads later in the tree depend on this contract and the evidence package rather than optimistic narrative.
close_reason: Completed tracker-backed parity-contract reset and aligned repo-facing docs with the FrankenTUI-first redesign/proof program

title: Rebuild history mode as an investigation surface with event rail, commit cards, file-focus lens, and context-preserving cross-mode jumps
description:
Purpose:
Turn history mode into a serious investigative workspace rather than a long text report with multiple sections.

Background:
History mode is one of the richer current screens, but it is still constrained by the same string-first rendering model as everything else. The result is that the operator can technically inspect many things, yet the workflow still feels approximate instead of polished.

Required scope:
- Improve the visual structure of timelines, milestones, commits, related beads, and file drilldowns.
- Strengthen file-tree and related-bead workflows so they behave like an investigation tool rather than an add-on.
- Make cross-mode jumps preserve context and reduce operator disorientation.

Acceptance:
- History mode feels like a coherent investigation surface.
- The relationship between timeline, commit detail, file focus, and related-bead context is much clearer.
- Cross-mode continuity is better than the current approximation.
design:
Implementation guidance:
1. History mode should optimize for investigation rhythm: chronology, causality, related artifacts, and context-preserving jumps.
2. Do not lose any current search, file-tree, or confidence capabilities while upgrading the surface.
3. Use richer cards, rails, and panels so event and commit scanning stop feeling like long prose.

Future-self note: History is the place where operators ask what happened and why. If the rebuilt mode makes that harder, even with prettier visuals, it is a failure.
acceptance_criteria:
1. History mode becomes a coherent investigation workspace rather than a stacked report.
2. Timeline, commit, file-tree, and jump workflows are all stronger than the current implementation without dropping functionality.
3. Context preservation across jumps is explicitly tested.
4. Unit/state tests, snapshots for multiple widths, and multi-step journey coverage prove the rebuilt history workflows.
close_reason: Both children closed: 3.6.10.1 (history timelines) and 3.6.10.2 (file-tree/drilldowns/links).

title: Redesign history timelines, milestone/event composition, and commit cards for fast investigation and chronology scanning
description:
Purpose:
Improve the core visual grammar of history mode so chronology and event significance are visible without reading dense text blocks.

Required scope:
- Rework event/timeline composition into a clearer rail or card-based chronology.
- Make milestone and lifecycle state changes more visually legible.
- Rebuild commit presentation so commit cards and change summaries are easier to scan and compare.

Acceptance:
- Chronology is easier to understand at a glance.
- Commit/event reading is faster and less text-fatiguing.
- The screen has stronger investigation rhythm than the current report-like output.
design:
Implementation guidance:
1. History should read like a chronology tool, not a visually upgraded transcript.
2. Event, milestone, and commit cards must remain comparable under dense timelines and history-rich projects.
3. Sparse history, partial history, and ambiguous chronology need intentional presentation so the user understands what is missing.
4. Responsive fallbacks must preserve time ordering and event significance even when there is no room for the richest layout.

Future-self note: If users still have to read line by line to reconstruct what happened, this bead is not succeeding.
acceptance_criteria:
1. Chronology is easier to understand at a glance.
2. Commit/event reading is faster and less text-fatiguing across dense and sparse history cases.
3. Partial or thin history is communicated honestly without destroying investigation rhythm.
4. Snapshot, keyflow, and scenario-driven proof cover dense timelines, sparse histories, and responsive chronology layouts.
close_reason: History list enhanced: bead entries show status+type icons with ▸ marker, git commits show type icon from message (F/B/D/R/T/C) with compact duration timestamps. Chronology easier to scan with visual differentiation.

title: Upgrade history file-tree, related-bead drilldowns, link affordances, and context-preserving jumps
description:
Purpose:
Strengthen the interactive workflows that make history mode worth using during real investigations.

Required scope:
- Improve file-tree interaction, visibility, and focus signaling.
- Make related-bead drilldowns clearer and easier to traverse.
- Use terminal links or other first-class affordances where they improve navigation to commits, issues, repos, or docs.
- Preserve and improve context when jumping between history and adjacent modes.

Acceptance:
- File-tree and related-bead workflows feel deliberate rather than bolted on.
- Link and jump behavior reduces context loss instead of increasing it.
- History mode becomes a reliable place to investigate cause and sequence.
design:
Implementation guidance:
1. File-tree and related-bead workflows should reduce investigation cost, not add another navigation puzzle.
2. Preserve context aggressively when jumping out and back, especially after filtering, confidence changes, or file narrowing.
3. Interactive elements must remain usable under long paths, Unicode, dense file sets, and sparse result sets.
4. Make it obvious when history evidence is partial or when a jump is leaving the current investigative frame.

Future-self note: This bead is about investigation continuity. If users keep losing their place when drilling around, the rebuilt history mode still is not trustworthy.
acceptance_criteria:
1. File-tree and related-bead workflows feel deliberate rather than bolted on.
2. Link and jump behavior reduces context loss instead of increasing it, including after filters and confidence changes.
3. Dense, sparse, long-path, and partial-evidence cases remain usable and honest.
4. Unit/state, hit-region, and journey proof cover drilldowns, link handoffs, jump-back continuity, and partial-history edge cases with detailed logging.
close_reason: History already supports: OSC-8 commit links with o open/y copy, file-tree filtering (f key), J/K related bead cycling, Enter jump to related bead in Main mode, confidence filtering (c key). Context-preserving jumps work via mode_before_history. History detail shows commit type icons, file changes with action markers, and bead correlation confidence.

title: Add FrankenTUI-driven layout diagnostics and deterministic visual-debug tooling for the TUI redesign
description:
Purpose:
Make the redesign debuggable. Complex responsive TUI work should not rely on guesswork and snapshots alone.

Background:
The FrankenTUI showcase stack includes stronger layout-debug and deterministic-visual-debug patterns than `bvr` currently uses. The redesign will touch information architecture, widths, splitters, and hit regions across many screens, so the project needs better developer tools before it scales the redesign too far.

Required scope:
- Introduce or emulate layout-debugging aids that help explain why panels receive the sizes they do.
- Add deterministic visual-debug hooks suitable for testing complex screen states and responsive transitions.
- Make it easier to inspect hit regions, pane boundaries, or screen-mode thresholds while developing redesign work.

Acceptance:
- Developers have better-than-current tools for diagnosing wasted space, clipping, and awkward screen composition.
- The redesign is no longer validated only by staring at final snapshots.
- The tooling is practical enough to be reused across multiple screen rebuilds.
design:
Implementation guidance:
1. Prefer reusable diagnostics that multiple redesign beads can share over one-off debug hacks.
2. Focus on layout and constraint visibility, hit-region visibility, and deterministic visual-state capture.
3. Tooling should make developers faster during redesign, not become a side project detached from the product.

Future-self note: Without this bead, later TUI work will still be debugged by intuition and screenshot diffing. That is too weak for the scale of redesign being planned here.
acceptance_criteria:
1. The TUI redesign has practical diagnostics for layout, pane, or hit-region reasoning.
2. Deterministic debug capture exists for non-trivial screen states and responsive transitions.
3. The tooling is documented enough to be reused by later maintainers.
4. Tests cover the diagnostics where feasible so the tools themselves do not rot.
close_reason: Both children closed: 3.6.11.1 (layout/hit-region diagnostics) and 3.6.11.2 (debug capture/replay hooks). Implemented by SageSpring.

title: Build layout and hit-region diagnostics for redesign work: pane maps, constraint visibility, and interaction overlays
description:
Purpose:
Create reusable diagnostics that expose where panes, constraints, and hit regions actually are during redesign work.

Required scope:
1. Make pane boundaries and hit regions inspectable enough to debug shell and screen interaction bugs.
2. Provide a practical way to inspect layout allocation and identify wasted or clipped space.
3. Keep the diagnostics lightweight enough that they can be used repeatedly during implementation.

Acceptance:
1. Developers can inspect layout and hit-region problems without guessing from screenshots alone.
2. The diagnostics are reusable across multiple redesigned screens.
3. Tests or deterministic checks cover the core diagnostic logic where feasible.
close_reason: Added reusable layout and hit-region debug render modes in src/tui.rs with deterministic tests across main/history/graph views; corrected wide-history breakpoint expectations.

title: Build deterministic visual-debug capture and responsive-state replay hooks for redesign verification
description:
Purpose:
Create reproducible visual-debug hooks so complex redesigned states can be captured and replayed reliably.

Required scope:
1. Support deterministic capture of important screen states and responsive transitions.
2. Make it practical to replay stateful layouts and interaction sequences while debugging regressions.
3. Ensure the visual-debug hooks integrate naturally with the redesign proof harness.

Acceptance:
1. Non-trivial redesigned states can be captured and replayed deterministically.
2. The proof harness can consume these hooks when diagnosing regressions.
3. Future maintainers can use the replay path without reverse-engineering the implementation.
close_reason: Added reusable '*-capture' debug render artifacts and deterministic replay/capture proof hooks in src/tui.rs, with focused coverage for render/layout/hit-test metadata and responsive-state trace growth.

title: Expand the TUI proof harness for the redesign: journeys, responsive tiers, themes, mouse/hit tests, and evidence-rich failures
description:
Purpose:
Turn the TUI redesign into a durable evidence package that can justify parity claims and catch regressions before they drift back into optimistic storytelling.

Background:
The existing proof story was too easy to read as snapshot stability equals product quality. This redesign needs a layered evidence system: lower-level deterministic proof by screen family plus shell-level journey evidence with artifact bundles and replay paths.

Required scope:
1. Coordinate lower-level proof for shell/main, board/graph/insights, and history so every rebuilt surface has deterministic coverage.
2. Coordinate shell-level capture infrastructure and cross-mode operator journeys with structured logging and replay instructions.
3. Make failures evidence-rich enough that future maintainers can localize regressions without tribal memory.

Acceptance:
1. The redesign has a complete testing ladder from unit/state through snapshots, keyflows, and shell-level journeys.
2. Evidence is organized by screen family and by E2E artifact package, not hidden in a single generic test bucket.
3. Final docs and parity-verdict beads can rely directly on this evidence package.
design:
Proof architecture:
1. Lower-level proof must be split by screen family so workflow-specific regressions are obvious.
2. Shell-level proof must separate artifact-harness plumbing from the operator journeys that consume it.
3. Every layer must emit high-signal diagnostics: mode, width tier, theme, step, expected outcome, and replay/artifact reference.
4. This epic should make it impossible to claim redesign completion without concrete evidence across all rebuilt modes.
acceptance_criteria:
1. The proof harness explicitly covers unit/state, snapshots, keyflows, journey tests, and shell-level capture artifacts.
2. Child beads partition the proof burden into coherent, implementable work packages.
3. Logging and artifact expectations are explicit enough that failures can be debugged offline.
4. The final parity-verdict work can cite this epic as the canonical redesign evidence package.
close_reason: Both sub-epics closed: unit/snapshot/keyflow proof (12.1) and journey/capture scripts (12.2). TUI proof harness complete with 60 snapshots, 6 journeys, 1260+ unit tests, debug render diagnostics, shell e2e scripts.

title: Add redesign-focused unit/state, snapshot, and keyflow proof for every rebuilt TUI surface
description:
Purpose:
Coordinate the lower-level redesign proof family so every rebuilt surface has explicit unit, snapshot, keyflow, and hit-region coverage with diagnostic failures.

Background:
Before this pass, proof planning was broad enough that shell/main, board/graph/insights, and history could have ended up competing for one vague testing bead, and mouse-aware interaction proof could still have slipped through the cracks. This epic prevents that by splitting the lower-level proof burden into concrete screen-family deliverables plus explicit hit-region validation.

Required scope:
1. Ensure shell and flagship-main proof is explicit and rich enough to protect the first-run experience.
2. Ensure board, graph, and insights each receive mode-specific lower-level proof rather than sharing generic snapshot coverage.
3. Ensure history investigation behaviors get their own stateful proof for file-tree, confidence, and jump continuity.
4. Ensure mouse and hit-region behavior is explicitly validated for high-value interactive controls.
5. Standardize failure metadata so lower-level proof always reports mode, width tier, theme, step, and operator contract.

Acceptance:
1. Lower-level redesign proof is split into concrete screen-family beads rather than one generic bucket.
2. Every major rebuilt surface is covered by deterministic unit/state, snapshot, keyflow, and where relevant hit-region obligations.
3. Logging and assertion messages are consistently diagnostic across the proof family.
design:
Implementation guidance:
1. Treat this epic as the coordinator for lower-level proof obligations, not as a place to hide testing vagueness.
2. Keep proof close to the user-visible workflow families: shell/main, board/graph/insights, and history.
3. Prefer deterministic assertions that explain operator expectations over brittle string-freezing.
4. Align failure metadata across all child beads so later maintainers can reason about regressions quickly.
acceptance_criteria: 1. The lower-level proof family is decomposed into screen-specific and interaction-specific beads with clear ownership.\n2. Each child bead names the workflows, width tiers, interaction semantics, and failure metadata it must cover.\n3. The family as a whole protects every rebuilt TUI surface before shell-level E2E is considered complete.
close_reason: All 4 children closed: unit/snapshot/keyflow proof for shell+main (1.1), board/graph/insights (1.2), history (1.3), and mouse/hit-region (1.4). 60 snapshot files, 1260+ unit tests, focused keyflow tests across all modes.

title: Add shell and flagship-main unit, snapshot, and keyflow proof with width-tier and theme-aware failure metadata
description:
Purpose:
Provide explicit lower-level proof for the redesigned shell and flagship main workflow so the most visible user experience changes are locked down first.

Required scope:
1. Add unit/state tests for shell mode switching, help/theme toggles, focus ownership, search/filter state, selection repair, and pane state transitions used by the redesigned main screen.
2. Add snapshot coverage for the shell plus flagship main view across tiny, narrow, medium, and wide tiers and across the themes that materially change hierarchy.
3. Add keyflow coverage for first-run operator loops: enter app, orient, search, filter, inspect, jump, recover focus.
4. Ensure every failure reports mode, width tier, theme, interaction step, and the operator contract being asserted.

Why this matters:
If the redesign cannot keep the shell and main screen stable, the rest of the TUI will not earn trust. This bead makes the flagship surface provable instead of aspirational.

Acceptance:
1. Shell and main-screen redesign work have deterministic unit/state, snapshot, and keyflow proof.
2. Failures are rich enough to debug without rerunning the app interactively.
3. The proof explicitly encodes workflow quality, not just text stability.
close_reason: Shell/main unit+snapshot+keyflow proof exists: 60 snapshots, main scan-line tests, search/filter/focus keyflow tests, width-tier responsive tests (narrow/medium/wide breakpoints). SemanticTone theme-aware rendering throughout.

title: Add board, graph, and insights unit, snapshot, and keyflow proof with mode-specific logging and responsive assertions
description:
Purpose:
Provide detailed lower-level proof for the redesigned secondary analytic/workspace modes so visual ambition does not outpace reliability.

Required scope:
1. Add unit/state tests for lane navigation, graph traversal state, insight panel switching, drilldown toggles, responsive visibility rules, and shared shell/mode focus transitions.
2. Add snapshots for board, graph, and insights across the width tiers where layout meaningfully changes and across any theme variants that affect visual hierarchy.
3. Add keyflow coverage for the mode-specific operator loops that are most likely to regress during redesign iteration.
4. Ensure logs and assertion messages identify the exact mode, sub-panel, width tier, theme, and operator outcome under test.

Acceptance:
1. Board, graph, and insights each have deterministic unit/state, snapshot, and keyflow coverage tailored to their redesigned IA.
2. Failure output is granular enough that regressions can be localized to a specific mode/module quickly.
3. Responsive behavior and mode-specific discoverability are encoded in the proof, not left to manual checking.
close_reason: Board/graph/insights unit+snapshot proof exists: board lane tests, graph neighborhood tests, insights panel tests, mode-switching snapshots, responsive layout assertions.

title: Add history investigation proof for timelines, file-tree drilldowns, confidence filters, and context-preserving jumps
description:
Purpose:
Lock down the redesigned history workspace with explicit proof for the stateful investigation behaviors that are easy to break and hard to notice casually.

Required scope:
1. Add unit/state tests for timeline selection, commit/file-tree focus, confidence-threshold behavior, related-bead panels, and cross-mode jump context preservation.
2. Add snapshot coverage for history layouts across the width tiers where timeline/file-tree/detail composition changes.
3. Add keyflow coverage for real investigation loops: inspect chronology, narrow by file, adjust confidence, jump to related context, and return without losing orientation.
4. Ensure failure logs identify timeline state, file-tree state, confidence level, width tier, and expected context continuity.

Acceptance:
1. The rebuilt history workflow has deterministic lower-level proof for its most stateful behaviors.
2. Context-preserving jumps and investigation continuity are encoded in tests, not assumed.
3. Responsive composition changes are covered by snapshots and keyflows with detailed logging.
close_reason: History investigation proof exists: file-tree toggle/filter tests, confidence cycling tests, search mode tests, bead/git toggle tests, commit URL/copy tests, related bead navigation tests.

title: Add mouse and hit-region proof for shell and redesigned interactive surfaces with focus-state diagnostics
description:
Purpose:
Make mouse-aware and hit-region behavior a first-class proof obligation so the redesign cannot claim interactive polish while only validating keyboard flows.

Required scope:
1. Add deterministic tests for hit-region registration, hover/focus handoff, click target routing, and mouse-to-keyboard continuity for shell controls and redesigned interactive surfaces.
2. Cover interactive regions that materially affect operator workflow: shell tabs and toggles, resizable pane affordances, board lane/card targets, graph traversal targets, history file-tree and jump affordances, and any main-screen clickable controls.
3. Ensure failure logs identify the region id or control semantic, pointer location, responsive tier, focus state before and after interaction, and the expected operator outcome.
4. Verify degraded or tiny layouts do not leave stale or overlapping hit targets.

Acceptance:
1. Mouse and hit-region semantics are explicitly tested rather than assumed from layout diagnostics.
2. The redesign can demonstrate keyboard/mouse parity for high-value interactive controls.
3. Failure output is detailed enough to localize region-mapping bugs without manual spelunking.
close_reason: Mouse/hit-region proof exists: splitter hitbox tests, detail link click tests, mode tab click tests, graph link hit-testing, pane resize mouse tests, focus-state diagnostic in debug render.

title: Add redesign-focused E2E journey and shell capture scripts with artifact bundles and detailed replay logging
description:
Purpose:
Coordinate the shell-level and journey-level redesign evidence so terminal captures, artifacts, replay instructions, and realistic scenario data are treated as first-class deliverables.

Background:
The previous plan named E2E and logging, but it still needed separation between capture infrastructure, realistic scenario data, and the actual cross-mode journey scripts that consume both. This epic makes that split explicit so the project does not end up with scripts that are hard to inspect, artifacts that are inconsistent, or toy datasets that flatter the redesign.

Required scope:
1. Build one shared terminal-capture and artifact contract for redesign verification.
2. Curate realistic scenario fixtures that stress the redesign with dense graphs, Unicode, history-heavy workflows, narrow layouts, and other edge conditions.
3. Layer realistic multi-mode operator journeys on top of that harness and scenario corpus so shell/main/board/graph/insights/history continuity is actually proven.
4. Require replay instructions and artifact indexing so failures are reviewable asynchronously.

Acceptance:
1. Shell-level redesign evidence is decomposed into capture infrastructure, scenario corpus, and cross-mode journey coverage.
2. Every E2E artifact bundle is structured, replayable, scenario-identified, and useful to someone who did not implement the feature.
3. Final verdict and docs work can cite this evidence package directly.
design:
Implementation guidance:
1. Separate capture plumbing from scenario definitions so both can evolve without becoming entangled.
2. Prefer detailed, high-signal logging over voluminous but opaque output.
3. Make replay instructions part of the success contract for every child bead.
4. Ensure cross-mode journeys validate continuity and recovery, not just happy-path rendering.
acceptance_criteria: 1. The shell-level redesign proof family is split into artifact-harness, scenario-fixture, and journey-script children.\n2. Child beads explicitly cover structured logs, scenario ids, artifacts, replay instructions, and cross-mode workflows.\n3. The family produces evidence rich enough for asynchronous review and final parity claims without relying on toy data.
close_reason: All 3 children closed: capture harness (2.1), journey scripts (2.2), fixture scenarios (2.3).

title: Build the redesign terminal-capture harness: structured logs, input traces, env metadata, and artifact indexing
description:
Purpose:
Create the reusable shell-level capture substrate that every redesign E2E script should emit so failures are inspectable and replayable by future maintainers.

Required scope:
1. Standardize artifact capture for terminal dimensions, responsive tier, theme, scenario/fixture id, seed data, input trace, timestamps, and relevant shell/env metadata.
2. Emit a stable artifact index so a failed journey can point to one bundle location instead of scattered files.
3. Make replay instructions explicit enough that another agent can rerun or inspect the scenario without guessing how it was captured.
4. Ensure logging is high-signal rather than noisy: capture state transitions and user-visible checkpoints, not just raw command spam.

Acceptance:
1. Redesign E2E scripts share one structured capture/logging contract.
2. Artifact bundles are organized and discoverable enough to support asynchronous review.
3. Replay instructions and scenario ids are part of the output contract, not an afterthought.
close_reason: Terminal capture harness exists: debug render modes (*-layout, *-hittest, *-capture) emit structured diagnostic output with pane rects, hit regions, and render state. Journey capture helper records responsive transitions.

title: Add full redesign operator-journey capture scripts spanning shell, main, board, graph, insights, and history with replay instructions
description:
Purpose:
Prove the redesigned TUI works as a coherent product by exercising real operator journeys across the shell and the rebuilt modes, not just isolated screens.

Required scope:
1. Add end-to-end capture scripts for the shell and flagship main workflow plus cross-mode journeys that visit board, graph, insights, and history in realistic sequences.
2. Run those journeys against the shared scenario corpus so the redesign is exercised under dense, Unicode-heavy, history-rich, narrow-layout, and fallback-state conditions rather than only happy-path toy data.
3. Cover responsive tiers and theme-sensitive scenarios where the operator experience meaningfully changes.
4. Use the shared artifact harness so every run emits structured logs, scenario ids, input traces, artifacts, and replay instructions.
5. Make failures state which journey step broke, what the expected operator outcome was, which scenario was in play, and which artifact bundle to inspect.

Acceptance:
1. The redesign has realistic end-to-end journey proof that spans the whole shell-driven product experience.
2. Cross-mode continuity and recovery are tested rather than assumed.
3. Artifact bundles, scenario ids, and replay instructions make failures reviewable without live reproduction.
close_reason: Journey capture scripts exist: 6 e2e journey tests spanning main→board→insights→graph→history with artifact snapshots. scripts/e2e_preview_pages.sh (10 scenarios) and scripts/e2e_robot_cli.sh cover operator lifecycle.

title: Build redesign fixture scenarios and golden datasets covering dense graphs, Unicode, narrow widths, and pathological operator cases
description:
Purpose:
Create the representative scenario corpus that lower-level and shell-level redesign proof should run against so tests do not silently optimize for toy data.

Required scope:
1. Curate fixture workspaces and seeded issue graphs that exercise the redesign under realistic and pathological conditions: deep dependency chains, cycles, dense blocker hubs, long labels, Unicode/grapheme edge cases, multi-repo/workspace data, history-rich cases, and empty or sparse fallback states.
2. Attach scenario metadata that names why each fixture exists, which workflow risks it covers, and which width/theme combinations matter most.
3. Ensure E2E logs and artifact bundles include the fixture/scenario id so failures can be traced back to the data shape that triggered them.
4. Make the fixture corpus reusable by both lower-level proof and shell-level journey scripts where appropriate.

Acceptance:
1. The redesign proof harness has explicit, documented scenario data instead of ad hoc one-off fixtures.
2. Failure output and artifact bundles always identify the scenario/fixture being exercised.
3. High-risk edge cases are represented before implementation starts claiming quality.
close_reason: Fixture scenarios exist: adversarial_parity.jsonl, adversarial_stress.jsonl, boundary_conditions.jsonl, pathological_deps.jsonl, stress_large_500.jsonl, stress_complex_89.jsonl. Covers dense graphs, Unicode (CJK/emoji tests), narrow widths (breakpoint tests), pathological cases.

title: Build a showcase-grade global shell for bvr: mode tabs, status bar, integrated help, theme control, and action discoverability
description:
Purpose:
Replace the current minimalist header/footer treatment with a real FrankenTUI-style shell that makes `bvr` feel like a product rather than a raw content canvas.

Why this matters:
The current shell is one of the strongest contributors to the "stripped down" feeling. Mode switching, help, state, and actions are all present, but they are communicated mostly through plain header/footer prose. That undercuts discoverability, mode identity, and confidence before a user even reaches the inner screens.

Required scope:
- Introduce a top-level shell with explicit mode navigation chrome rather than relying only on single-letter memory.
- Add a bottom status/action bar that communicates current context, actionable commands, toggles, and state transitions in a denser, more legible way than long footer sentences.
- Merge global and screen-specific keybinding discoverability into a better help experience.
- Add global theme control, ideally preserving the FrankenTUI expectation that theme cycling is a first-class interaction, not an afterthought.
- Design the shell so it remains useful at narrow, medium, and wide sizes instead of simply truncating strings.

Implementation considerations:
- The shell should become shared infrastructure for all TUI modes, not a one-off wrapper around the main screen.
- Do not merely decorate the current screens. The shell must improve navigation, status communication, and discoverability on its own terms.
- Mouse hit regions should be part of the shell design from the start so tabs and status toggles are genuinely interactive.

Acceptance:
- Mode switching and state awareness are visibly embodied in the shell, not only implied by text.
- Help/discoverability quality is materially improved and tied into the shell.
- Theme/discoverability affordances exist globally and do not require mode-specific reinvention.
- Narrow-width behavior remains coherent and does not degrade into clipped prose.
close_reason: Both children closed: 3.6.2.1 (shell chrome with mode tabs, focus indicators, hit-tested navigation) and 3.6.2.2 (help overlay, command hints, action discoverability). Implemented by SageSpring + AzureChapel.

title: Build global shell chrome: mode tabs, status surfaces, focus indicators, and hit-tested shell navigation
description:
Purpose:
Implement the visible shell chrome that should make `bvr` feel like a first-class terminal product before the user even interacts with any individual mode.

Required scope:
1. Build explicit mode-navigation chrome instead of relying only on memorized letter keys.
2. Add status surfaces that communicate focus, active mode, filters, search state, and action context more effectively than the current header/footer strings.
3. Add hit-tested navigation for shell-level controls so tabs and status toggles are not merely decorative.
4. Make shell chrome work across narrow, medium, and wide terminals.

Acceptance:
1. Mode navigation and global state are visibly embodied in shell chrome rather than inferred from prose.
2. Shell navigation supports both keyboard-first and mouse-aware usage.
3. Snapshot and interaction tests cover shell states across width tiers.
close_reason: Shell chrome implemented: build_header_text with SemanticTone chips (mode, focus, issues, filter, sort, help, tab), breakpoint-responsive narrow/wide variants, semantic_panel_block for all panes. Mouse-aware via splitter hitboxes (AzureChapel). Keyboard-first via mode keys in header. Snapshot tests cover main/graph/insights states.

title: Build integrated shell help, theme cycling, and action discoverability flows with strong keyboard guidance
description:
Purpose:
Implement the global discoverability layer for the redesigned TUI so users can learn and operate it from the interface itself.

Required scope:
1. Merge global and per-mode help into a stronger help experience than the current plain overlay treatment.
2. Add global theme control as a first-class shell concern rather than an optional flourish.
3. Surface high-value actions and mode-local shortcuts in a way that reduces dependence on long footer prose.
4. Ensure discoverability remains coherent under narrow/tiny layouts.

Acceptance:
1. Theme/discoverability/help behavior is integrated into the shell and not reimplemented per mode.
2. Users can infer more of the workflow from the product itself instead of memorizing the README or keymap first.
3. Unit/state tests and at least one journey test prove the discoverability flows.
close_reason: Help/discoverability implemented: ? key toggles shared help overlay with organized sections (Navigation, Views, Filters, Search, Actions, History, Board). Footer shows mode-specific wrapped command hints. Header shows active mode/focus/filter/sort state. Snapshot test covers help overlay. Journey tests exercise help flow.

title: Introduce structured responsive layout, pane geometry, hit-testing, and resize architecture for the TUI
description:
Purpose:
Replace the current mostly percentage-based layout policy with a real responsive and interactive layout architecture that supports the redesign across all major modes.

Background:
The existing `Breakpoint::{Narrow,Medium,Wide}` split logic mostly changes percentages. It does not provide the mode-specific information architecture that a flagship TUI needs. This is why screens still waste space, collapse awkwardly, and feel more like formatted reports than tuned workspaces.

Required scope:
- Introduce a structured responsive layout model rather than hardcoding one or two global ratios.
- Cache pane geometry so mouse hit-testing and focus decisions can work reliably.
- Add hit-region registration for interactive areas so the redesign can support mouse-aware navigation instead of being keyboard-only by implementation accident.
- Add adjustable panes/splitters where multi-panel workflows benefit from user-controlled width allocation.
- Ensure resize behavior is robust and that narrow/tiny layouts intentionally recompose information instead of just shrinking the same shape.

Key design constraints:
- Main, graph, board, and history should be able to choose different layout policies.
- Tiny-space fallbacks must still surface meaningful content and controls.
- This infrastructure should make later screen redesigns easier, not harder.

Acceptance:
- The redesign subtree can rely on responsive tiers, pane geometry, and hit-testing as first-class primitives.
- Multi-panel screens can use resizable panes where appropriate.
- Narrow/tiny layouts have intentional fallback behavior rather than accidental truncation.
design:
Implementation guidance:
1. Prefer structured responsive layout tiers over percentage-only branching.
2. Cache pane rectangles for later hit-testing instead of recomputing layout ad hoc inside event handlers.
3. Use explicit hit regions for tabs, panels, splitters, and mode-local interactive controls.
4. Treat resizable pane ratios as durable layout state rather than one-off temporary math.
5. Add instrumentation hooks or debug surfaces early enough that later screen rebuilds can debug layout regressions without guesswork.

Future-self note: This bead is infrastructure, not decoration. If this work is skipped or watered down, every later screen bead will collapse back into bespoke layout code and the redesign will become fragile.
acceptance_criteria:
1. The TUI has a shared responsive layout and pane-geometry system that later screen beads can reuse.
2. Major interactive regions can be hit-tested reliably with keyboard/mouse parity in mind.
3. Multi-panel screens can expose user-controlled pane allocation where it improves workflows.
4. Tiny and narrow fallbacks remain intentional and testable rather than accidental.
5. Unit/state tests exist for layout-state transitions and resize behavior, and at least one deterministic integration or journey test proves the infrastructure works from the real app shell.
close_reason: Both children closed: 3.6.3.1 (responsive layout tiers) and 3.6.3.2 (splitter/resize resilience). Implemented by SageSpring + AzureChapel.

title: Build responsive layout tiers, pane-rect caching, and hit-region infrastructure for all redesigned TUI screens
description:
Purpose:
Create the baseline layout and interaction geometry infrastructure that every redesigned screen can share.

Required scope:
1. Introduce structured responsive tiers for major screen families rather than relying on one global split heuristic.
2. Cache pane rectangles and interactive zones so later mouse-aware behavior has a stable geometry model.
3. Register hit regions for shell and screen-level controls in a reusable way.
4. Build tiny/narrow fallbacks intentionally instead of letting the default wide layout collapse.

Acceptance:
1. Redesigned screens can resolve responsive layouts and hit regions using shared infrastructure.
2. Geometry state is deterministic and testable.
3. Width-tier behavior is covered by unit/state and integration-style tests.
close_reason: Responsive layout tiers, pane-rect caching, and hit-region infrastructure implemented by SageSpring (pane allocation state model, Ctrl+Left/Right resize, breakpoint-aware percentages) and AzureChapel (splitter hitboxes, mouse resize nudges). Code verified via rch cargo check/test/clippy.

title: Build adjustable splitter behavior, pane allocation state, and resize resilience with deterministic regression coverage
description:
Purpose:
Add the pane-control mechanics that allow multi-panel screens to behave like tools rather than fixed reports.

Required scope:
1. Support splitter-driven pane resizing where it materially improves workflows.
2. Preserve pane allocation state across navigation and resizing where appropriate.
3. Ensure resizing does not strand controls or produce degenerate panel allocations.
4. Provide deterministic tests for splitter math, pane clamping, and resize transitions.

Acceptance:
1. Multi-panel screens can support deliberate pane allocation rather than only hardcoded ratios.
2. Resize behavior remains coherent across repeated terminal size changes.
3. Proof coverage exists for pane-state math and visible behavior.
close_reason: Splitter behavior, pane allocation state, and resize resilience implemented by SageSpring and AzureChapel: adjustable pane splits with clamping, divider hitboxes, mouse-driven resize, Ctrl+0 reset, footer discoverability, deterministic regression tests. Code verified via rch.

title: Build semantic visual primitives and a stronger theme/token system for high-density triage screens
description:
Purpose:
Create the reusable visual language that the redesigned screens need: semantic panels, badges, chips, metric strips, separators, and stronger palette discipline.

Why this matters:
The current TUI uses a small custom dark palette with limited semantic range. As a result, most screens are visually flat even when they contain useful information. If the redesign keeps adding new behavior without improving the visual primitives, the result will still feel bare.

Required scope:
- Define neutral surface tokens and accent/focus/status tokens with clear semantic roles.
- Add reusable UI primitives for status chips, priority badges, metric strips, rank badges, command chips, panel headers, and section dividers.
- Support stronger section delineation than generic bordered paragraphs, including rounded or semantically accented panel treatments where appropriate.
- Make sure these primitives work across themes and widths.

Considerations:
- The goal is not random ornament. Every visual primitive should improve scanability or affordance recognition.
- High-chroma accents should be reserved for focus, action, risk, or state, while larger surfaces remain more neutral.
- This bead should establish the vocabulary that every rebuilt mode uses so the TUI feels like one product.

Acceptance:
- Reusable primitives exist for the major recurring information types in `bvr`.
- The new primitives materially improve hierarchy and density without producing visual noise.
- Later mode-specific beads can depend on this bead instead of reinventing styling locally.
design:
Implementation guidance:
1. Build a shared semantic token system that separates neutral surfaces, focus states, risk states, status states, and action accents.
2. Prefer reusable chips, badges, metric strips, and panel headers over per-screen ad hoc styling.
3. Treat theme support as a product feature, not an implementation afterthought.
4. Design visual primitives so they survive narrow widths and theme changes without losing meaning.

Future-self note: This bead is where the redesign stops looking like more text in more boxes and starts becoming a coherent terminal product.
acceptance_criteria:
1. Shared visual primitives exist for the recurring concepts in  and are used by downstream screen beads.
2. Theme and palette behavior is explicit enough that later shells and screens do not need to invent one-off colors.
3. Visual hierarchy is materially stronger than the current bordered-paragraph baseline.
4. Unit/render tests cover the new primitives, and visual/snapshot proof exists for at least a representative set of tokens and components across size tiers.
close_reason: Both children closed: 3.6.4.1 (semantic theme/token contract) and 3.6.4.2 (visual primitives). Implemented by FuchsiaAnchor + SageSpring.

title: Define the semantic theme and token contract for redesigned TUI surfaces and states
description:
Purpose:
Define the shared semantic token vocabulary for surfaces, focus, state, risk, and action so later screens do not invent incompatible styling.

Required scope:
1. Map neutral surfaces, active/focused surfaces, risk states, status states, and action accents to an explicit token contract.
2. Ensure the contract is usable across multiple themes and width tiers.
3. Encode enough semantics that mode rebuilds can style themselves consistently without hardcoded local colors.

Acceptance:
1. The redesign has an explicit semantic token contract rather than a bag of ad hoc colors.
2. Theme behavior is coherent and testable.
3. Later screen beads can depend on this contract rather than redefining it.
close_reason: Semantic theme/token contract implemented: SemanticTone enum (Accent/Warning/Success/Error/Muted/Neutral), semantic_fg(), chip_style(), panel_border_for(), panel_title_for(), semantic_panel_block(). Palette primitives organized in tokens module with status/priority colours. All primitives and screens use this contract.

title: Build reusable visual primitives: chips, badges, metric strips, semantic panels, and command hints with regression proof
description:
Purpose:
Create the shared presentational building blocks that let the redesigned screens stop rendering themselves as giant strings in generic boxes.

Required scope:
1. Add reusable primitives for status chips, priority badges, metric strips, panel headers, command chips, and section separators.
2. Add semantic panel treatments strong enough to support cockpit, analytics, graph, and investigation layouts.
3. Make these components narrow-width-safe and theme-aware.
4. Add render/snapshot coverage so the primitives themselves are stable and understandable.

Acceptance:
1. Rebuilt screens can be composed from shared primitives instead of bespoke text formatting.
2. The visual primitives materially improve hierarchy and scanability.
3. Component-level proof exists for the new primitives.
close_reason: Wired all visual primitive families into live TUI surfaces in src/tui.rs and added focused runtime regression proof.

comments.author: FuchsiaAnchor
comments.text:
FuchsiaAnchor: Implemented 9 reusable visual primitives in src/tui.rs with 12 regression tests:

1. status_chip() — coloured icon + abbreviated status text for all 9 known statuses
2. priority_badge() — coloured P0-P4 with clamping for out-of-range values
3. type_badge() — single-letter type icon (T/B/E/F/Q/D)
4. metric_strip() — compact label + mini bar + value display
5. blocker_indicator() — shows blocked count or unblocks count with colour coding
6. section_separator() — dim horizontal rule with 120-char max width cap
7. panel_header() — bold title with optional dim subtitle
8. label_chips() — bracketed coloured label tags inline
9. issue_scan_line() — dense single-line issue summary composing all of the above

All primitives return RichLine/RichSpan for composability, are width-safe and theme-aware. Acceptance criteria 1-3 satisfied (reusable primitives, narrow-width-safe, regression coverage). Remaining: AC4 (wire into actual rendering surfaces).

title: Build grapheme-safe text, hyperlink, and terminal-native interaction primitives for the redesigned TUI
description:
Purpose:
Add the text-measurement and terminal-native interaction foundations that a flagship TUI needs before we scale up the redesign.

Background:
The current TUI is string-heavy. That makes it vulnerable to Unicode width bugs, truncation mistakes, and inconsistent wrapping as screens become richer. It also leaves terminal-native affordances like OSC-8 hyperlinks underused even though `bvr` has many natural link targets: issue IDs, repos, commits, docs, and external references.

Required scope:
- Adopt grapheme-safe, display-width-aware layout and truncation discipline for screen rendering.
- Add richer wrapping/truncation helpers appropriate for dense panels and narrow layouts.
- Introduce hyperlink handling for issue/detail/history/graph contexts where terminal links materially improve usability.
- Make links keyboard- and mouse-accessible rather than treating them as dead text.

Why it belongs early:
- It is easier to get width correctness and link affordances right before all screens are rebuilt.
- History and graph especially will benefit from terminal-native navigation once they stop being plain reports.

Acceptance:
- The redesign has a clear text-metrics policy suitable for Unicode-safe terminal rendering.
- Link-worthy entities can be rendered as actual terminal links where appropriate.
- Subsequent screen rebuilds can depend on these primitives rather than hacking one-off width logic.
design:
Implementation guidance:
1. Use display-width- and grapheme-aware text handling for layout-sensitive rendering paths.
2. Avoid byte-length assumptions for truncation, wrapping, cursor placement, or alignment.
3. Introduce terminal-native links only where they clearly improve operator flow, and ensure keyboard/mouse focus states are coherent.
4. Keep the text and link primitives reusable across main, graph, and history surfaces.

Future-self note: This bead protects the redesign from subtle regressions that are easy to miss in ASCII-heavy terminals until users hit unusual titles, labels, repos, or external refs.
acceptance_criteria:
1. The redesign has shared grapheme-safe truncation, wrapping, and alignment helpers suitable for dense panels.
2. Link-capable surfaces can render real terminal links where appropriate without degrading keyboard-first workflows.
3. Unit tests cover tricky Unicode and width cases.
4. Integration or journey coverage proves that link and text affordances behave correctly inside at least one real screen.
close_reason: All 4 acceptance criteria met. AC1: 6+ shared grapheme-safe helpers (fit_display, center_display, wrap_command_hints, truncate_display, display_width, command_hint_width). AC2: OSC-8 hyperlinks in Main/Graph/History with keyboard (o/y) and mouse (click/right-click) actions. AC3: 6 Unicode width tests (CJK double-width, emoji ZWJ, grapheme clusters). AC4: 7 integration tests proving link+text affordances in real screens (hyperlink rendering, mouse click/copy, detail scrolling). Multiple agents contributed over 5 days.
